4. **AB Quadrature with 1x/2x/4x Evaluation:** Same as above, but the HSC counts on selected edges — providing 1, 2, or 4 counts per pulse cycle for higher resolution.

**CV = PV Interrupt Mechanism:**

The HSC continuously compares the **Current Value (CV)** with a user-defined **Preset Value (PV)**. The instant CV becomes equal to PV, the HSC **immediately** triggers a hardware interrupt, calling an Interrupt OB (e.g., OB40 in Siemens S7-1200). This interrupt is serviced **before the main scan cycle**, ensuring zero-latency response. In a **cut-to-length** system, the encoder feeds length pulses; PV is set to the desired cut length. When CV = PV, the interrupt fires and **de-energizes the cutter solenoid** — all within microseconds, far faster than the scan-based alternative. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $PPR = 2500$, Mode = **4x**, $RPM = 1200$, $PV = 6000$ counts.

**(i) Encoder Output Frequency:**

$$
f = \frac{RPM \times PPR}{60} = \frac{1200 \times 2500}{60} = \frac{3{,}000{,}000}{60} = 50{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 50 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(ii) Counts per Revolution (4x mode):**

$$
N_{4x} = 4 \times PPR = 4 \times 2500 = 10{,}000 \text{ counts/rev}
$$

**(iii) Angular Resolution:**

$$
\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{10{,}000} = 0.036°/\text{count}
$$

**(iv) Angular Displacement for PV = 6000 counts:**

$$
\theta = \frac{PV}{N_{4x}} \times 360° = \frac{6000}{10{,}000} \times 360° = 0.6 \times 360° = 216°
$$

**Tabulated Result:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Output Frequency | $f$ | **50,000 Hz (50 kHz)** |
| Counts per rev | $N_{4x}$ | **10,000 counts/rev** |
| Angular resolution | $\theta_{res}$ | **0.036°/count** |
| PV displacement | $\theta$ | **216°** |

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) HSC Programming in Siemens S7-1200 (7 marks)**

The S7-1200 supports **6 HSC channels** (HSC1–HSC6), each with dedicated inputs (I0.0–I0.5). The programming procedure is:

**Step 1 — Hardware Configuration (Device Configuration in TIA Portal):**
- Add the CPU to the project.
- In the device view, select the CPU → **Properties → High Speed Counters (HSC)**.
- Enable the desired HSC (e.g., HSC1).
- Choose the **Operating Mode** (e.g., "Count external").
- Assign inputs: I0.0 (clock A), I0.1 (clock B), I0.2 (Z reset).
- Set the **count range** (e.g., 0 to 100,000).
- Enable **CV = PV interrupt** and **Direction reversal** features.

**Step 2 — Configuration Block (Data Block, e.g., "HSC_Config"):**
- The wizard creates a System Data Block (SDB) or a user DB with tags:
  - `CV`: Current Value
  - `PV`: Preset Value
  - `Direction`: Up/Down
  - `Interrupt_Event_OB`: OB40 (or similar)

**Step 3 — CTRL_HSC Instruction (FB):**
The CTRL_HSC block is called in OB1 to **modify HSC parameters at runtime** — e.g., changing PV, direction, or resetting CV. Inputs include HSC ID, new CV, new PV, and new direction.

**Step 4 — Interrupt OB (e.g., OB40):**
When CV = PV, the PLC calls OB40. The user writes code inside OB40 to take action (e.g., stop the cutter, fire the solenoid).

**Step 5 — Reading CV in Main Program:**
The CV register (e.g., `ID1000`) is read in OB1 to display, log, or use in calculations. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $PPR = 3600$, Mode = **2x**, $N_{counts} = 12{,}000$ in $t = 0.5$ s.

**(i) Counts per Revolution (2x mode):**

$$
N_{2x} = 2 \times PPR = 2 \times 3600 = 7200 \text{ counts/rev}
$$

**(ii) Angular Displacement Represented by 12,000 counts:**

$$
\theta = \frac{N_{counts}}{N_{2x}} \times 360° = \frac{12{,}000}{7200} \times 360° = \frac{5}{3} \times 360° = 600°
$$

(Note: 600° = 1 full revolution + 240°, indicating the shaft rotated more than one full turn.)

**(iii) Motor Speed in RPM:**

First, calculate the **encoder output frequency**:

$$
f = \frac{N_{counts}}{k \times t} = \frac{12{,}000}{2 \times 0.5} = 12{,}000 \text{ Hz}
$$

Then, calculate RPM using the standard formula:

$$
RPM = \frac{f \times 60}{PPR} = \frac{12{,}000 \times 60}{3600} = \frac{720{,}000}{3600} = 200 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Counts per rev (2x) | **7200** |
| Angular displacement | **600°** |
| Motor speed | **200 RPM** |

**(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up frequency vs. counts/second:** Students often write $f = N_{4x}/t$ instead of $f = N_{counts} / (k \times t)$. The $k$ factor (1, 2, or 4) MUST divide the count rate to recover the encoder's true output frequency.
> 2. **Confusing PPR with counts/rev:** PPR is on **one channel**. Total counts depend on decode mode. Always state both.
> 3. **Forgetting the 1/rev factor:** RPM is calculated from $f$ (channel A frequency) and $PPR$, NOT from counts/second directly.
> 4. **Not mentioning interrupt priority:** When asked about HSC advantages, students forget the **CV = PV interrupt** feature — a guaranteed 3-mark question every cycle.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High Speed Counter (HSC):**

- **Definition:** Hardware counter that operates independent of PLC scan time.
- **Why needed:** Standard counters limited to ~50 Hz by Nyquist; HSCs support up to 100–200 kHz.
- **Operating Modes:** Single-phase up, Single-phase up/down, Two-phase quadrature (1x, 2x, 4x).
- **Quadrature:** A and B 90° phase-shifted; direction by lead-lag; 4x gives maximum resolution.
- **Key Registers:** CV (Current Value), PV (Preset Value), Direction, Reset/Z.
- **Key Formulae to Memorize:**
  - $f = (RPM \times PPR) / 60$
  - $N_{4x} = 4 \times PPR$
  - $\theta_{res} = 360° / N_{counts/rev}$
  - $RPM = (f \times 60) / PPR$
  - $L_{res} = (\pi \cdot D) / N_{counts/rev}$
- **Critical Feature:** CV = PV hardware interrupt (zero-latency response).
- **Z-Phase:** Once-per-revolution marker pulse used for homing/absolute reference.
- **Filter:** Input digital filter (0.1 µs to 100 µs) used to debounce noisy signals.
- **Applications:** Cut-to-length, RPM measurement, position control, packaging, CNC, conveyors.
- **Common Trap:** Frequency is independent of decode mode — only the count granularity changes.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — High Speed Counter**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PWM and PID Blocks in PLC
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT831 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PWM and PID Blocks in PLC

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) in PLC

**Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) is a digital-to-analog conversion technique implemented within the PLC's instruction set, where the **duty cycle** of a fixed-frequency square wave is varied proportionally to a control input. The PLC produces a stream of digital ON/OFF pulses whose average DC voltage over one period equals the desired analog output level.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition Highlight:** PWM is *not* a true analog signal — it is a **time-averaged** analog equivalent produced by rapidly switching a digital output between HIGH and LOW. The controlled load (motor, heater, valve) responds to the *average* power, not the instantaneous switching.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine you are tapping your foot on a table to control the speed of a ceiling fan. If you tap fast and press hard 50% of the time and release 50% of the time, the fan spins at roughly half speed. Tapping harder/longer (higher duty cycle) = faster fan. Tapping briefly (low duty cycle) = slower fan. The PLC's PWM block does the same — it "taps" the output ON and OFF at a fixed rate, but the *proportion* of ON-time controls the effective power.

**Standard PWM Metrics (Industry Conventions):**

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Period** | $T$ | **0.1 ms to 65.5 s** (PLC dependent) |
| **Duty Cycle** | $D$ | **0% to 100%** |
| **Resolution** | $N$ bits | **8-bit to 16-bit** typical |
| **Frequency** | $f$ | **0.015 Hz to 10 kHz** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU's Industrial Automation context, PWM blocks in PLCs (e.g., Siemens S7-1200's `CTRL_PWM`, Allen-Bradley's `PWM` instruction, Schneider's `PWM` function block) are critical for **variable speed drives, solenoid proportional valves, DC motor controllers, and heater control**.

---

### 1.2 PID (Proportional–Integral–Derivative) Block in PLC

**Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A PID block is a **closed-loop feedback control function** embedded in the PLC's instruction library that continuously computes a corrective output based on three terms: the **Proportional** term (response to current error), the **Integral** term (response to accumulated past error), and the **Derivative** term (response to the rate of change of error). The block adjusts a control variable (e.g., valve opening, motor speed) to drive a process variable (PV) toward a desired setpoint (SP).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition Highlight:** The error signal in a PID block is conventionally defined as:
> $$e(t) = SP - PV$$
> where SP is the **Setpoint** (desired value) and PV is the **Process Variable** (measured value from a sensor).

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of PID as three different advisors giving driving directions to a car driver trying to reach a destination:
- **P (Proportional) — "How far are we?"** The further from the destination, the harder you press the accelerator.
- **I (Integral) — "How long have we been off-track?"** If you've been lagging for a long time, give a sustained extra push to eliminate that persistent offset.
- **D (Derivative) — "How fast are we closing in?"** Slow down the accelerator as you approach the destination to avoid overshooting.

A perfect driver uses all three intuitions together — and so does a PLC's PID block.

**PID Gain Parameters (Industry Standard Symbols):**

| Gain | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Proportional Gain** | $K_p$ | **Dimensionless or in %/unit** |
| **Integral Gain** | $K_i$ | **In 1/s (inverse seconds)** |
| **Derivative Gain** | $K_d$ | **In s (seconds)** |
| **Integral Time** | $T_i$ | **In seconds** |
| **Derivative Time** | $T_d$ | **In seconds** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 PWM Block — Operational Theory

#### 2.1.1 Working Principle (Structured Logic)

A PWM block in a PLC performs the following logical sequence each scan cycle:

1. **Accepts a control input** (typically a normalized value 0.0 to 1.0, or an integer 0 to 2ⁿ⁻¹ where *n* is resolution).
2. **Compares** the input with an internal counter that ramps from 0 to the maximum count (period value).
3. **Sets the output HIGH** when counter < input, and **LOW** when counter ≥ input.
4. **Resets the counter** at the end of each period $T$, and the cycle repeats.
5. The load (motor/heater) integrates the resulting pulsed waveform over time and responds to the **average value**.

#### 2.1.2 The 'Why' Behind PWM in Industrial Automation

- **Why not true analog?** Analog output modules in PLCs are expensive, drift with temperature, and have limited resolution (e.g., 12-bit ≈ 4096 steps).
- **Why PWM?** Digital outputs are cheap, immune to noise, and can drive high-power MOSFETs/IGBTs. A 1-bit digital output with PWM achieves smooth proportional control at low cost.
- **Real-world utility:** Variable frequency drives (VFDs), DC motor speed control, solenoid dosing pumps, LED brightness control, heater temperature control, servo valve positioning.

---

### 2.2 PID Block — Operational Theory (Three-Term Analysis)

#### 2.2.1 The Proportional Term ($P$)

- **What it does:** Produces an output proportional to the instantaneous error.
- **Equation:**
$$u_P(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)$$
- **Effect:** Larger error → larger corrective action. Acts like a spring.
- **Limitation:** Steady-state offset (residual error) always remains if P is used alone.

#### 2.2.2 The Integral Term ($I$)

- **What it does:** Accumulates past errors over time and produces an output that grows as long as any non-zero error persists.
- **Equation:**
$$u_I(t) = K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau = \frac{K_p}{T_i} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau$$
- **Effect:** Eliminates steady-state error. Acts like a memory.
- **Limitation:** Causes **integral windup** if not properly anti-wound (clamping the integrator when output saturates).

#### 2.2.3 The Derivative Term ($D$)

- **What it does:** Reacts to the *rate of change* of error — predicts future error.
- **Equation:**
$$u_D(t) = K_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt} = K_p \cdot T_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$
- **Effect:** Provides damping, reduces overshoot, improves stability.
- **Limitation:** Amplifies measurement noise (because differentiation of noise is severe).

#### 2.2.4 Combined PID Output (Parallel Form — Most Common in PLCs)

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** PLC PID blocks (e.g., Siemens FB41 "CONT_C", Allen-Bradley PIDE) are often implemented in **discrete-time (positional) form**:
> $$u_n = K_p \cdot e_n + K_i \cdot T_s \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k + \frac{K_d}{T_s} \cdot (e_n - e_{n-1})$$
> where $T_s$ is the **scan/sampling time** and $e_{n-1}$ is the previous error sample.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T} \times 100\%$ | Duty Cycle of PWM | **%** (dimensionless) |
| 2 | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{peak}$ | Average output voltage | **Volts (V)** |
| 3 | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | PWM frequency | **Hz** |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{2^{N}}$ | Output resolution (smallest step) | **V/step** |
| 5 | $e(t) = SP - PV$ | Error signal | **Process units** |
| 6 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | PID parallel form | **Control units** |
| 7 | $K_i = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}$ | Integral gain relation | **1/s** |
| 8 | $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$ | Derivative gain relation | **s** |
| 9 | $u_n = K_p e_n + K_i T_s \sum e_k + \dfrac{K_d}{T_s}(e_n - e_{n-1})$ | Discrete PID (positional) | **Control units** |
| 10 | $\Delta u_n = K_p(e_n - e_{n-1}) + K_i T_s e_n + \dfrac{K_d}{T_s}(e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2})$ | Incremental (velocity) PID | **Δu per scan** |
| 11 | $K_p = 0.6 K_u$ (Z-N) | Ziegler-Nichols P-only gain | **—** |
| 12 | $T_i = 0.5 T_u$ (Z-N) | Ziegler-Nichols integral time | **s** |
| 13 | $T_d = 0.125 T_u$ (Z-N) | Ziegler-Nichols derivative time | **s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions:** The PID output $u(t)$ is usually clamped between $u_{min}$ and $u_{max}$ (output saturation limits) to prevent damage to actuators. Anti-windup is mandatory whenever the actuator can saturate.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PWM:** Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs), brushless DC motor (BLDC) speed controllers, proportional solenoid valves in hydraulic systems, dimming systems, induction heating control.
- **PID:** Temperature control (furnaces, ovens, plastic extruders), pressure control (boilers, hydraulics), level control (tanks, reservoirs), flow control (chemical dosing, water treatment), position control (servo systems), pH control in process industries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: PWM Average Voltage and Power

**Problem:** A PWM signal has $T_{on} = 6$ ms ON and $T_{off} = 4$ ms OFF, with peak voltage $V_{peak} = 24$ V DC. Find the duty cycle, period, frequency, and average voltage delivered to a resistive load.

**Step 1 — Identify the given quantities.**

$$T_{on} = 6 \text{ ms}, \quad T_{off} = 4 \text{ ms}, \quad V_{peak} = 24 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total period $T$.**

$$T = T_{on} + T_{off} = 6 \text{ ms} + 4 \text{ ms} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the duty cycle $D$.**

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{6 \text{ ms}}{10 \text{ ms}} \times 100\% = 60\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute the frequency $f$.**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}} = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the average voltage $V_{avg}$ delivered to the load.**

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{peak} = 0.60 \times 24 \text{ V} = 14.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

A motor receiving this PWM signal behaves *as if* it is being supplied with 14.4 V DC, even though it is actually being switched between 24 V and 0 V at 100 Hz. This is the foundation of all low-cost DC motor speed control.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: PID Step Response with Given Gains

**Problem:** A temperature control loop has SP = 200 °C, initial PV = 180 °C. Gains: $K_p = 4$ %/°C, $K_i = 0.5$ %/(°C·s), $K_d = 0.1$ %·s/°C. Sampling time $T_s = 0.5$ s. Compute the controller output for the first **two** scans using the discrete (positional) PID formula, assuming $e_{n-1} = 0$ and the integral sum starts at 0.

**Step 1 — Write the discrete PID equation.**

$$u_n = K_p \cdot e_n + K_i \cdot T_s \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k + \frac{K_d}{T_s} \cdot (e_n - e_{n-1})$$

**Step 2 — Compute the initial error $e_0$.**

$$e_0 = SP - PV_0 = 200 - 180 = 20 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the controller output at scan $n = 0$.**

Substitute $e_0 = 20$, $e_{n-1} = 0$, $\sum e = 20$:

$$u_0 = 4 \times 20 + 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 20 + \frac{0.1}{0.5} \times (20 - 0)$$

$$u_0 = 80 + 5 + 4 = 89\%$$

**Step 4 — Assume the PV rises to 188 °C by scan 1 (process responds).**

$$e_1 = 200 - 188 = 12 \text{ °C}$$

Cumulative error sum:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{1} e_k = 20 + 12 = 32 \text{ °C·s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the controller output at scan $n = 1$.**

$$u_1 = K_p \cdot e_1 + K_i \cdot T_s \cdot \sum e + \frac{K_d}{T_s} \cdot (e_1 - e_0)$$

$$u_1 = 4 \times 12 + 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 32 + \frac{0.1}{0.5} \times (12 - 20)$$

$$u_1 = 48 + 8 - 1.6 = 54.4\%$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

The controller output **decreased** from 89% → 54.4% because:
- **P term** dropped (error halved).
- **I term** grew slightly (accumulating history).
- **D term** is **negative** because error is *decreasing* rapidly (rate of change is negative), so the controller eases off to prevent overshoot.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Ziegler-Nichols Ultimate Gain Tuning

**Problem:** A process becomes marginally (critically) oscillatory with $K_u = 10$ and period $T_u = 4$ s under P-only control. Compute the recommended P, PI, and PID gains using the Ziegler-Nichols open-loop/ultimate-cycle tuning rules.

**Step 1 — Recall the Z-N ultimate-cycle formulas.**

| Controller | $K_p$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | $0.5 \, K_u$ | — | — |
| **PI** | $0.45 \, K_u$ | $0.8 \, T_u$ | — |
| **PID** | $0.6 \, K_u$ | $0.5 \, T_u$ | $0.125 \, T_u$ |

**Step 2 — Compute P-only gain.**

$$K_p = 0.5 \times 10 = 5$$

**Step 3 — Compute PI gains.**

$$K_p = 0.45 \times 10 = 4.5$$

$$T_i = 0.8 \times 4 = 3.2 \text{ s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{4.5}{3.2} = 1.406 \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute PID gains.**

$$K_p = 0.6 \times 10 = 6$$

$$T_i = 0.5 \times 4 = 2 \text{ s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_i = \frac{6}{2} = 3 \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

$$T_d = 0.125 \times 4 = 0.5 \text{ s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_d = K_p \cdot T_d = 6 \times 0.5 = 3 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

These gains give a **quarter-amplitude-damped** response. In practice, they are taken as a starting point and then **manually fine-tuned** for the actual plant. The KTU examiner typically expects the formulas, not the final numbers.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — P, I, D Effect Summary

| Parameter | Rise Time | Overshoot | Settling Time | Steady-State Error | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Increase $K_p$** | Decreases ↑ | **Increases** ↑ | Slight increase | Decreases | **Degrades** ↑ |
| **Increase $K_i$** | Decreases ↑ | **Increases** ↑ | **Increases** ↑ | **Eliminates** ↑ | **Degrades** ↑ |
| **Increase $K_d$** | Minor change | **Decreases** ↑ | Decreases ↑ | No effect | **Improves** ↑ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** "Increase $K_p$" → faster but more oscillatory. "Add $I$" → kills offset but causes windup. "Add $D$" → dampens oscillation but amplifies sensor noise. The art of PID tuning lies in balancing these trade-offs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PWM Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Reference Value 0-100 percent] --> B{PWM Block in PLC}
    B --> C[Internal Counter 0 to Period]
    C --> D{Compare Counter vs Input}
    D -->|Counter less than Input| E[Digital Output = HIGH 24V]
    D -->|Counter greater or equal| F[Digital Output = LOW 0V]
    E --> G[Power Switch MOSFET or IGBT]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Industrial Load Motor or Heater or Solenoid]
    H --> I[Average Power proportional to Duty Cycle]
    J[Period T set by PLC config] --> C
    K[Clock Pulse from PLC] --> C
```

**Description of Flow:**
The PWM block reads a **reference value** (analog input, HMI slider, or computed setpoint), compares it against a **ramping counter** clocked at a fixed frequency, and drives a digital output HIGH or LOW accordingly. The downstream **MOSFET/IGBT switch** chops the DC supply into the load. The mechanical/thermal inertia of the load (motor, heater) filters the pulses into a smooth analog-like response.

---

### 4.2 PID Closed-Loop Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SP[Setpoint SP desired value] --> SUM[Subtractor comparator]
    PV[Process Variable PV measured] --> SUM
    SUM -->|Error e equals SP minus PV| PID[PID Block in PLC]
    PID -->|P Term proportional gain times e| PTERM[P]
    PID -->|I Term integral of e over time| ITERM[I]
    PID -->|D Term derivative of e| DTERM[D]
    PTERM --> SUM2[Summing junction]
    ITERM --> SUM2
    DTERM --> SUM2
    SUM2 -->|Control Output u bounded by Umin Umax| ACT[Actuator Valve or Heater or Motor]
    ACT --> PROC[Industrial Process Tank or Furnace]
    PROC -->|Process response| SENSOR[Sensor Thermocouple or Pressure Transmitter]
    SENSOR -->|Feedback signal| PV
    SAT[Anti-windup Clamp] -.->|Limit integrator| PID
```

**Description of Flow:**
The **Setpoint** (desired value) and **Process Variable** (measured value) are fed into a **subtractor** to produce the error. The PID block computes the three correction terms in parallel, sums them, and outputs a control signal that drives the **actuator**. The actuator changes the **process**, which is measured by a **sensor** and fed back. The **anti-windup clamp** prevents the integrator from accumulating unbounded error when the actuator saturates.

---

### 4.3 PWM Timing Diagram (Block-Level Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PERIOD[One PWM Period T equals 10 ms]
        T1[On Time 6 ms] --> T2[Off Time 4 ms]
    end
    PERIOD --> DUTY[Duty Cycle D equals 60 percent]
    DUTY --> AVG[V average equals 0.6 times V peak equals 14.4 V at 24V supply]
```

---

### 4.4 PID Step-Response Behavior (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step Input Setpoint] --> B{PID Tuned}
    B -->|Underdamped Small Kd| C[Oscillatory response overshoot and ringing]
    B -->|Critically Damped Optimal Kp Ki Kd| D[Fast rise no overshoot minimum settling]
    B -->|Overdamped Large Kd| E[Slow rise no overshoot long settling]
    B -->|P only| F[Fast rise but persistent offset]
    B -->|PI only| G[Eliminates offset but may overshoot]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) as used in PLC-based industrial automation. List any two industrial applications of PWM.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
PWM is a digital modulation technique in which the **duty cycle** of a fixed-frequency square wave (pulse train) is varied in proportion to a control input, thereby producing a time-averaged analog-like output from a digital PLC output. **(2 marks)**

Industrial applications: **(any two, ½ mark each)**
1. Variable speed control of DC / brushless DC motors.
2. Proportional control of solenoid valves (hydraulic/pneumatic).
3. Temperature control of heating elements.
4. LED intensity control and dimming systems.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Write the mathematical expression for the output of a PID controller. Briefly explain the role of the integral term.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The output of a PID controller in parallel form is: **(1 mark)**

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Role of the integral term:** The integral term continuously sums up (accumulates) all past errors. As long as any non-zero error exists, the integral term grows and drives the controller output, thereby **eliminating the steady-state (offset) error** that a purely proportional controller cannot remove. However, it can cause **integral windup** if the actuator saturates, which is why anti-windup schemes are mandatory. **(2 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly lose marks by writing the **continuous-time** PID equation when PLCs operate in **discrete-time**. Always state the scan time $T_s$ explicitly.
> - Do **not** confuse $K_i = K_p / T_i$ with $K_i$ as a separate gain — this relation is *examined*.
> - In Ziegler-Nichols questions, do not forget the **0.125** factor in $T_d$ for PID.
> - For PWM questions, do not say "PWM produces an analog output" — it is a **time-averaged** effect; the output is fundamentally digital.
> - Always draw a **closed-loop block diagram** for PID questions to score full marks on "explain with diagram".

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — PWM Focus**

**(a) Explain the working principle of a PWM block in a PLC with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for average output voltage in terms of duty cycle.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A PWM block generates a digital pulse train of fixed period $T$ and variable pulse width $T_{on}$, such that the **duty cycle** $D = T_{on}/T$ is proportional to the analog control input.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Input 0 to 100 percent] --> B[Comparator inside PLC]
    C[Counter 0 to Period] --> B
    B --> D[Digital Output PWM Wave]
    D --> E[Load Motor or Heater]
```

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Let $V_{peak}$ be the supply voltage (e.g., 24 V DC). The PWM waveform is $V_{peak}$ during $T_{on}$ and $0$ V during $T_{off}$. The average voltage over one period is:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t)\,dt = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T_{on}} V_{peak}\,dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0\,dt \right]$$

$$V_{avg} = \frac{V_{peak} \cdot T_{on}}{T} = D \cdot V_{peak}$$

Since $D$ ranges from 0 to 1, $V_{avg}$ ranges from 0 to $V_{peak}$, achieving full analog-like control from a single digital output.

---

**(b) A PLC generates a PWM signal with period $T = 20$ ms and $T_{on} = 8$ ms. The supply is 24 V DC, and the load is a 10 Ω resistive heater. Calculate (i) duty cycle, (ii) frequency, (iii) average voltage, and (iv) average power dissipated.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Duty Cycle (1 mark):**
$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{8}{20} \times 100\% = 40\%$$

**(ii) Frequency (1 mark):**
$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{20 \times 10^{-3}} = 50 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iii) Average Voltage (2 marks):**
$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{peak} = 0.40 \times 24 = 9.6 \text{ V}$$

**(iv) Average Power (3 marks):**
$$P_{avg} = \frac{V_{avg}^2}{R} = \frac{(9.6)^2}{10} = \frac{92.16}{10} = 9.216 \text{ W}$$

(Equivalently, $P_{avg} = D \cdot V_{peak}^2 / R = 0.40 \times 576 / 10 = 23.04$ W — note: these are NOT equal; the correct $V_{avg}^2/R$ formula is the time-averaged heating power, which differs from $D \cdot P_{peak}$.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — PID Focus**

**(a) With a neat closed-loop block diagram, explain the operation of a PID block in a PLC. Derive the expression for the discrete-time PID output.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> SUM[Summing Junction]
    PV[Process Variable PV from Sensor] --> SUM
    SUM -->|Error e equals SP minus PV| PID[PID Function Block]
    PID --> OUT[Control Output u bounded 0 to 100 percent]
    OUT --> ACT[Actuator]
    ACT --> PROC[Process]
    PROC --> SENSOR[Sensor]
    SENSOR --> PV
```

**Operation (2 marks):**
The PID block continuously samples the error $e_n = SP - PV_n$, computes three corrective actions (P, I, D), sums them, and outputs a control signal that drives the actuator. The actuator changes the process, which is sensed and fed back — forming a closed loop that drives the PV toward the SP.

**Derivation of Discrete PID (3 marks):**
Starting from the continuous form:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Discretize with sampling time $T_s$:
- Integral → backward rectangular sum: $\int \to T_s \sum e_k$
- Derivative → backward difference: $\frac{de}{dt} \to \frac{e_n - e_{n-1}}{T_s}$

Therefore:

$$u_n = K_p e_n + K_i T_s \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k + \frac{K_d}{T_s}(e_n - e_{n-1})$$

---

**(b) A temperature loop has SP = 150 °C. Initial PV = 130 °C. $K_p = 3$, $K_i = 0.4$ s⁻¹, $K_d = 0.05$ s, $T_s = 1$ s. Compute the PID output for the first two scans, assuming PV becomes 138 °C after scan 1 and $e_{n-1} = 0$ initially.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Scan 0 (n = 0) — 3 marks:**

$$e_0 = 150 - 130 = 20 \text{ °C}$$
$$\sum e = 20$$
$$u_0 = 3 \times 20 + 0.4 \times 1 \times 20 + \frac{0.05}{1} \times (20 - 0)$$
$$u_0 = 60 + 8 + 1 = 69\%$$

**Scan 1 (n = 1) — 3 marks:**

$$e_1 = 150 - 138 = 12 \text{ °C}$$
$$\sum e = 20 + 12 = 32$$
$$u_1 = 3 \times 12 + 0.4 \times 1 \times 32 + \frac{0.05}{1} \times (12 - 20)$$
$$u_1 = 36 + 12.8 - 0.4 = 48.4\%$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
The output dropped from 69% → 48.4% as the error shrank. The P term decreased, the I term increased (due to accumulated history), and the D term is negative because the error is *decreasing* rapidly — the controller is anticipating the approach to setpoint and easing off the actuator to prevent overshoot.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Read this the night before the exam!**

- **PWM = digital technique, time-averaged analog effect.** Always state duty cycle formula $D = T_{on}/T$ and $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{peak}$.
- **PWM block** is a single digital output. Frequency = 1/T. Resolution = smallest change in duty cycle.
- **PID is a closed-loop** controller. Error $e = SP - PV$. Output is the sum of P, I, D terms.
- **P-only** leaves steady-state offset. **I** removes offset but causes windup. **D** dampens oscillation but amplifies noise.
- **Three forms of PID:** Ideal parallel, standard ISA, and discrete (positional) — know the discrete form **cold**.
- **Relation:** $K_i = K_p/T_i$ and $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$. Do not confuse $K_i$ with $T_i$.
- **Anti-windup** is mandatory when the actuator can saturate. Use clamping or back-calculation.
- **Tuning methods:** Manual, Ziegler-Nichols (ultimate gain), Cohen-Coon. Z-N gives quarter-amplitude-damped response.
- **Z-N PID formulas:** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$ — *memorize exactly*.
- **PLC-specific blocks:** Siemens `CTRL_PWM` and `FB41 CONT_C`, Allen-Bradley `PWM` and `PIDE`, Schneider `PWM` and `PID_FB` — name-drop in answers for extra credit.
- **Always include units** in numerical answers: $K_i$ in 1/s, $K_d$ in s, $T_s$ in s, $V_{avg}$ in V.
- **Draw a block diagram** in every PID question — examiners award 2 marks for it.
- **Sampling time $T_s$** must be stated explicitly in discrete PID derivations.
- **Real-world link:** PWM → motor drives, heater choppers, LED dimmers. PID → temperature, pressure, level, flow, pH control in process industries.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLC Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT831 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PLC Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of PLC Design

**PLC Design** is the systematic engineering process of planning, selecting, configuring, and integrating a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) into an industrial automation system to reliably monitor inputs, execute user-defined control logic, and drive output actuators. It encompasses **hardware selection (CPU, I/O modules, power supply, memory)**, **software/ladder logic development**, **I/O addressing**, **wiring topology**, **safety interlocks**, and **commissioning procedures** in strict accordance with IEC 61131-3 and IEC 61131-2 standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OEICT831 - Module 3):**
> PLC Design covers the structured methodology of choosing a PLC for a given industrial process — from pre-design analysis (process study, I/O count, scan-time estimation) to final integration (panel layout, grounding, noise immunity, redundancy, and documentation).

### 1.2 Sub-Topics Under PLC Design

1. **PLC Selection Criteria** — Number/type of I/O, scan time, memory, communication.
2. **I/O Module Selection** — Digital vs. Analog, voltage levels, isolation.
3. **CPU & Memory Sizing** — Word size, program memory, data memory.
4. **Power Supply & Backplane Design** — Current budgeting, redundancy.
5. **Wiring & Panel Design** — Sink/Source, shielding, grounding, segregation.
6. **Safety & Hot-Swap Design** — Fail-safe, redundancy, diagnostics.
7. **Documentation Standards** — I/O list, ladder logic, HMI mapping, network topology.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Relay Cabinet" Analogy

Imagine you are wiring a **factory control panel by hand using dozens of electromechanical relays**, timers, and counters. Every time the process changes, you must rewire copper. Now imagine replacing that entire cabinet with **one compact digital "brain"** — the PLC.

- The **Input Terminals (I)** of the PLC act like the **"senses"** of the brain — eyes (sensors) and ears (switches).
- The **CPU + Memory** act like the **"thinking"** of the brain — running the rules you typed in.
- The **Output Terminals (O)** act like the **"muscles"** — motors, solenoids, and contactors.
- The **Ladder Logic Program** is the **"rulebook"** the brain follows, written in a graphical language resembling electrical rungs.

Just as an architect designs a building **before** laying bricks, a control engineer designs the PLC system **before** writing a single line of ladder logic. A poor design = catastrophic downtime, voltage noise, memory overflow, and safety hazards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology Cheat Sheet (for first-time readers):**
> - **Sink (NPN) Wiring** — Current flows *into* the PLC input terminal from the field device.
> - **Source (PNP) Wiring** — Current flows *out of* the PLC input terminal into the field device.
> - **Sink/Source** describes whether the I/O module *absorbs* or *supplies* current.
> - **Scan Time** — Total time for one complete read → execute → write cycle of the PLC.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in PLC Design (KTU Board-Weighted)

| Metric | Standard Unit | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| I/O Count | Points (Digital/Analog) | 16 – 8192 |
| Scan Time | milliseconds (ms) | 1 – 50 ms |
| Program Memory | Kilobytes (KB) / Steps | 2 – 4096 KB |
| Cycle Time | µs / Boolean instruction | 0.08 – 0.5 µs |
| I/O Update Time | ms | < 2 ms |
| Operating Temperature | °C | 0 – 55 °C |
| Power Supply | VDC / VAC | 24 VDC, 120/230 VAC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEC 61131-3** is the international standard for PLC programming languages. The five languages are **Ladder Diagram (LD), Function Block Diagram (FBD), Structured Text (ST), Instruction List (IL)**, and **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)**. KTU exams often expect knowledge of LD and FBD.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phased Methodology of PLC Design (KTU Board-Favorite)

PLC design is performed in **seven sequential phases**. Examiners love to test the *sequence* and *purpose* of each phase.

### Phase 1 — Process & Functional Specification
- Study the **Sequence of Operations (SOO)**, **P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram)**, and **Functional Design Specification (FDS)**.
- List **all sensors** (push buttons, limit switches, photoelectric, proximity, level, flow, pressure) and **all actuators** (contactors, solenoids, VFDs, valves, lamps).
- Define **control strategies** (continuous, batch, sequential).

### Phase 2 — I/O List Generation
- Create a tabular **I/O List** with columns: *Tag No., Description, I/O Type, Voltage, Signal Range, Module Slot, Terminal No., Remarks*.
- This is the **single most important deliverable** in PLC design. Without an I/O list, you cannot size a PLC.

### Phase 3 — PLC Selection
Selection criteria (in order of priority):
1. **Number of I/O points** → Choose CPU with at least 20% spare capacity.
2. **Type of I/O** → Digital (DI/DO), Analog (AI/AO), Specialty (RTD, Thermocouple, High-Speed Counter).
3. **Memory Size** → Based on program complexity.
4. **Scan Time** → Based on process speed.
5. **Communication Protocols** → Modbus, Profibus, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, EtherCAT.
6. **Environmental Ruggedness** → Temperature, humidity, vibration, IP rating.
7. **Redundancy Requirement** → Hot-Standby CPU for critical processes.
8. **Vendor Support & Lifecycle** → Spare availability ≥ 10 years.

### Phase 4 — Hardware Configuration
- **Backplane / Rack** sizing.
- **Power supply** current budgeting (sum of all module currents + CPU + 30% margin).
- **I/O module slot assignment** — group by voltage and signal type.
- **Terminal blocks** and **fuses**.

### Phase 5 — Wiring Design
- Segregate **AC power**, **DC power**, **analog signal**, and **communication** cables in separate ducts.
- Apply **shielding** for analog and communication cables, ground shield at **one end only**.
- Use **twisted pairs** for analog signals.

### Phase 6 — Safety & Diagnostics
- Implement **Emergency Stop (E-Stop)** hardwired in series with output contactors — never through software alone.
- Use **Safety PLCs (SIL 2/3)** for hazardous processes per **IEC 61508 / IEC 62061**.
- Add **watchdog timers** and **diagnostic LEDs**.

### Phase 7 — Documentation, Testing & Commissioning
- **FAT (Factory Acceptance Test)** → in vendor's facility.
- **SAT (Site Acceptance Test)** → at customer site.
- **I/O Loop Test**, **Ladder Logic Simulation**, **Cold Test**, **Hot Test**, **Load Test**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Most students lose marks by skipping Phase 2 (I/O list). The I/O list is the *bridge* between process engineering and control engineering — always draw it as a **markdown table in your answer** to earn full marks.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_{total} = N_{DI} + N_{DO} + N_{AI} + N_{AO} + N_{SP}$ | Total I/O count = sum of all categories | points |
| 2 | $N_{CPU} = N_{total} \cdot (1 + k_{spare})$ | CPU I/O capacity with spare margin (k = 0.2 typical) | points |
| 3 | $T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{comm}$ | Total scan time = sum of four phases | ms |
| 4 | $T_{program} = N_{inst} \cdot t_{inst}$ | Program scan time = instructions × per-instruction time | ms |
| 5 | $I_{PSU} = \sum I_{modules} \cdot (1 + k_{margin})$ | Power supply current with 30% margin | A |
| 6 | $M_{prog} = f(N_{I/O}, N_{timers}, N_{counters}, N_{flags})$ | Memory sizing (rough rule: 5–10 words per I/O point) | Words/KB |
| 7 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan} \cdot 2}$ | Maximum discrete input frequency (Nyquist limit) | Hz |
| 8 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{V_{supply} - V_{drop}}{I_{load}}$ | Minimum load resistance for analog outputs | Ω |
| 9 | $N_{rungs} \approx (0.5 \text{ to } 1.0) \cdot N_{DO}$ | Empirical rule for ladder program size | rungs |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KR-1 Anti-Ambiguity Rule (Mandatory):**
> In the markdown tables above, the **vertical pipe `\|`** is used **only** as a column separator. For mathematical absolute value, conditioning, or "such that" we use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ — never bare $\vert$. This prevents the markdown parser from breaking the table.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of PLC Design

- **Manufacturing plants** — discrete parts assembly lines.
- **Process industries** — oil & gas, chemical, water treatment.
- **Power plants** — turbine sequencing, generator protection.
- **Building automation** — HVAC, lighting, elevator control.
- **Automotive** — robotic welding, paint shops.
- **Food & Beverage** — batch mixing, CIP (Clean-In-Place) systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in Industry 4.0:** Modern PLC design now integrates **IIoT gateways**, **OPC UA servers**, **edge analytics**, and **cloud connectivity**. A 2024 KTU student must understand the *foundational* PLC design principles — they are the bedrock of all modern DCS and SCADA architectures.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Calculations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — PLC Sizing from a Given Process

**Problem Statement (Model-Question Style):**
A packaging line has the following field devices:

- 18 push buttons / limit switches (Digital Inputs, 24 VDC)
- 8 proximity sensors (Digital Inputs, 24 VDC)
- 4 pressure transmitters (Analog Inputs, 4–20 mA)
- 2 RTD temperature sensors (Analog Inputs, RTD module)
- 12 solenoid valves (Digital Outputs, 24 VDC, 0.5 A each)
- 6 motor contactors (Digital Outputs, 230 VAC)
- 3 VFD speed references (Analog Outputs, 4–20 mA)
- 1 communication port for HMI

**Required:** (a) Total I/O count, (b) PLC selection with spare margin, (c) Power supply sizing if each module draws 1.2 A on average, (d) Program memory estimate, (e) Maximum allowable scan time if a high-speed photo sensor must detect 2 kHz pulses.

---

### 3.1.1 Total I/O Count — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Sum all Digital Inputs**

$$N_{DI} = 18 \text{ (push buttons / limit switches)} + 8 \text{ (proximity)} = 26 \text{ points}$$

**Step 2: Sum all Digital Outputs**

$$N_{DO} = 12 \text{ (solenoids)} + 6 \text{ (contactors)} = 18 \text{ points}$$

**Step 3: Sum all Analog Inputs**

$$N_{AI} = 4 \text{ (pressure)} + 2 \text{ (RTD)} = 6 \text{ points}$$

**Step 4: Sum all Analog Outputs**

$$N_{AO} = 3 \text{ (VFD references)} = 3 \text{ points}$$

**Step 5: Total I/O count**

$$N_{total} = N_{DI} + N_{DO} + N_{AI} + N_{AO} = 26 + 18 + 6 + 3 = 53 \text{ points}$$

This is a **small-to-medium** PLC application. A **compact / modular PLC** with 64 I/O capacity is suitable.

---

### 3.1.2 PLC Selection with 20% Spare Margin

Applying the formula from the cheat sheet:

$$N_{CPU} = N_{total} \cdot (1 + k_{spare})$$

Substitute the values:

$$N_{CPU} = 53 \cdot (1 + 0.20)$$

$$N_{CPU} = 53 \cdot 1.20$$

$$N_{CPU} = 63.6 \approx 64 \text{ points}$$

**Conclusion:** Select a PLC with **64 I/O capacity** — for example, a **Siemens S7-1200 CPU 1214C DC/DC/DC** (14 DI / 10 DO onboard + 1 AI), expandable with **Signal Modules (SM)** to 64 points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reasoning behind 20% spare:** Future expansion (additional sensors, new safety requirements) and failed-channel isolation. A 10% margin is acceptable; 30% is preferred for fast-growing plants.

---

### 3.1.3 Power Supply Sizing

Given:
- 5 I/O modules (2× DI, 1× DO, 1× AI, 1× AO/RTD) each drawing **1.2 A** on average.
- CPU drawing **0.8 A**.

Applying the formula:

$$I_{PSU} = \left( \sum I_{modules} + I_{CPU} \right) \cdot (1 + k_{margin})$$

Substitute:

$$I_{PSU} = (5 \cdot 1.2 + 0.8) \cdot (1 + 0.30)$$

$$I_{PSU} = (6.0 + 0.8) \cdot 1.30$$

$$I_{PSU} = 6.8 \cdot 1.30$$

$$I_{PSU} = 8.84 \text{ A}$$

**Conclusion:** Select a **24 VDC, 10 A** power supply (next standard size up). Always oversize the PSU — under-rated PSUs cause voltage sag → module resets → plant trips.

---

### 3.1.4 Program Memory Estimate

Using the empirical rule $M_{prog} \approx 5$ to $10$ words per I/O point:

$$M_{prog} = 8 \text{ words/I/O} \cdot 53 \text{ I/O}$$

$$M_{prog} = 424 \text{ words}$$

Convert to KB (1 word = 2 bytes for most modern PLCs):

$$M_{prog} = 424 \cdot 2 = 848 \text{ bytes} \approx 0.85 \text{ KB}$$

Plus **commentary, symbols, and FB/FC libraries** (~3×):

$$M_{total} = 0.85 \cdot 3 \approx 2.55 \text{ KB}$$

**Conclusion:** A PLC with **50 KB program memory** has more than enough headroom. Most modern compact PLCs offer 75 KB – 4 MB.

---

### 3.1.5 Maximum Scan Time (Nyquist Criterion)

Given:
- High-speed photo sensor pulse train frequency: $f_{max} = 2$ kHz $= 2000$ Hz.

Applying the Nyquist sampling rule:

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{scan} \cdot 2}$$

Rearranging:

$$T_{scan} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot f_{max}}$$

$$T_{scan} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 2000}$$

$$T_{scan} = \frac{1}{4000}$$

$$T_{scan} = 0.00025 \text{ s} = 0.25 \text{ ms}$$

**Conclusion:** The PLC's scan time **must be ≤ 0.25 ms** to reliably detect 2 kHz pulses. This is **impossible** for a standard compact PLC (typical 1–10 ms). The solution is a **dedicated High-Speed Counter (HSC) module** that captures pulses *hardware-wise*, independent of the scan cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The scan-time limit is one of the *most commonly tested* concepts. Always remember: if the required scan is below the PLC's capability, use a **high-speed counter**, **interrupt-driven task**, or **special function module**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Wiring Design: Sink vs. Source

**Problem:** Decide whether a **PNP (sourcing) proximity sensor** can be connected to a **sinking (NPN) PLC input module**.

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1: Identify the sensor output type**
A PNP sensor **sources** current — current flows from the sensor's positive terminal **out** to the load.

**Step 2: Identify the PLC input type**
A sinking (NPN) PLC input **sinks** current — current flows from the field device, into the PLC terminal, then to common (0 V).

**Step 3: Compatibility check**

For current to flow, the circuit must be:

$$\text{(+) Supply} \rightarrow \text{Sensor (PNP)} \rightarrow \text{PLC Input (sinking)} \rightarrow \text{Common (0 V)} \rightarrow \text{Supply (−)}$$

**Conclusion:** PNP sensor + sinking input = **COMPATIBLE** ✓ (This is the **most common industrial configuration** — Euro/US standard).

The reverse combination (NPN sensor + sourcing input) is also valid but less common.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Ladder Logic Rung Count Estimate

**Problem:** A PLC system has 18 solenoids, 6 contactors, 12 timers, 8 counters, 4 PID loops. Estimate the **number of rungs** in the ladder program.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Output-driven rungs**

For each DO, at least one rung is needed. Some DO have start/stop logic (2 rungs each).

$$N_{rungs, DO} = 18 + 2 \cdot 6 = 30 \text{ rungs}$$

**Step 2: Timer and Counter rungs** — each TC requires:
- 1 rung to **enable** the timer/counter.
- 1 rung to **use** its done bit.
- Total: 2 rungs per TC.

$$N_{rungs, TC} = 2 \cdot (12 + 8) = 40 \text{ rungs}$$

**Step 3: PID rungs** — each PID loop typically uses **1 dedicated function block** plus enable + alarm logic (~3 rungs each).

$$N_{rungs, PID} = 3 \cdot 4 = 12 \text{ rungs}$$

**Step 4: Total rungs**

$$N_{rungs, total} = 30 + 40 + 12 = 82 \text{ rungs}$$

**Step 5: Add 25% margin for interlocks, alarms, and HMI logic**

$$N_{rungs, final} = 82 \cdot 1.25 \approx 103 \text{ rungs}$$

**Conclusion:** ~100 rungs is a **modest** ladder program. Any compact PLC can handle this easily.

---

## 3.4 KTU 2024 Style — Summary Analytical Matrix

| Design Parameter | Formula | Typical Value | KTU Board-Expected Mark Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total I/O count | $N_{total} = \sum N$ | Given in problem | 2 marks |
| Spare margin sizing | $N_{CPU} = N_{total} \cdot 1.2$ | 1.2× | 2 marks |
| Scan time limit | $T_{scan} \le \frac{1}{2 f_{max}}$ | ≤ 0.25 ms (for 2 kHz) | 3 marks |
| PSU current | $I_{PSU} = \sum I \cdot 1.3$ | Sum × 1.3 | 2 marks |
| Memory size | $M \approx 5\text{–}10$ words/I/O | 0.5 – 2 KB | 1 mark |
| Sink/Source compatibility | Match PNP↔Sink, NPN↔Source | — | 2 marks |
| Redundancy class | Hot-Standby / Warm-Standby / None | Process-critical → Hot | 1 mark |
| Safety integrity | SIL 1 / 2 / 3 per IEC 61508 | Hazardous → SIL 2/3 | 2 marks |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — PLC Hardware Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR["Power Supply 24 VDC or 230 VAC"] --> BACK["Backplane / Rack"]
    PWR --> CPU
    BACK --> CPU["CPU Module with Memory and Processor"]
    BACK --> DI["Digital Input Module DI 24 VDC"]
    BACK --> DO["Digital Output Module DO Relay or Transistor"]
    BACK --> AI["Analog Input Module AI 4-20 mA or RTD"]
    BACK --> AO["Analog Output Module AO 4-20 mA"]
    BACK --> HSC["High Speed Counter HSC Module"]
    BACK --> COMM["Communication Module Profinet Modbus"]
    CPU --> PG["Programming Device PC with TIA Portal or Step 7"]
    CPU --> HMI["HMI Touch Panel"]
    DI --> F1["Field Inputs Push Buttons Sensors Switches"]
    DO --> F2["Field Outputs Contactors Solenoids Valves"]
    AI --> F3["Field Transmitters Pressure Flow Level"]
    AO --> F4["Actuators VFD Control Valves Dampers"]
    COMM --> NET["Plant Network SCADA DCS Cloud"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Power supply feeds the backplane. The backplane distributes power and provides a parallel data highway to all I/O modules. The CPU executes the program stored in its memory, reads inputs via the backplane, processes logic, and writes outputs back via the backplane. Communication modules link the PLC to HMIs, SCADA, and the wider plant network.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — PLC Design Workflow (Phased Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Phase 1 Process and Functional Specification"] --> B["Phase 2 I/O List Generation"]
    B --> C["Phase 3 PLC Selection"]
    C --> D["Phase 4 Hardware Configuration"]
    D --> E["Phase 5 Wiring and Panel Design"]
    E --> F["Phase 6 Safety and Diagnostics"]
    F --> G["Phase 7 Documentation Testing and Commissioning"]
    G --> H["Plant Operation and Maintenance"]
    H -.->|"Feedback and Improvements"| A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for KTU exams:** The design is **iterative**, not strictly linear. Lessons learned in commissioning (Phase 7) feed back to refine the specification (Phase 1). This is the **V-Model** of automation engineering in miniature.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Sink vs. Source Wiring Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SINK["Sinking PLC Input NPN style"]
        V1["+24 VDC"] --> S1["PNP Sensor sources current"]
        S1 --> I1["PLC Input terminal sinks current"]
        I1 --> G1["0 V Common"]
    end
    subgraph SRC["Sourcing PLC Input PNP style"]
        V2["+24 VDC"] --> I2["PLC Input terminal sources current"]
        I2 --> S2["NPN Sensor sinks current to ground"]
        S2 --> G2["0 V Common"]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse sink and source. Memorize this rule: **"PNP sources — pair with SINKING input. NPN sinks — pair with SOURCING input."** This pairing ensures current always flows through the sensor's switching element.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — V-Model of PLC Engineering (Lifecycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    REQ["User Requirements Specification URS"] --> FS["Functional Specification FS"]
    FS --> DS["Design Specification DS"]
    DS --> HW["Hardware Build"]
    DS --> SW["Software Code Ladder Logic"]
    HW --> FAT["Factory Acceptance Test FAT"]
    SW --> FAT
    FAT --> SAT["Site Acceptance Test SAT"]
    SAT --> OP["Plant Operation"]
    DS -.->|"Verified by"| HW
    FS -.->|"Verified by"| SW
    REQ -.->|"Verified by"| FAT
    OP -.->|"Continuous Improvement"| REQ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering V-Model Principle:** Each specification level on the *left descending arm* is **verified** by the corresponding test on the *right ascending arm*. The URS is verified by SAT (final acceptance), the FS is verified by software tests, and the DS is verified by hardware build. This is a **board-favorite** topic for "Explain the V-Model in PLC engineering" type questions.
