- **OR Divergence (1.5 marks):** A **single horizontal line** placed on a vertical link. It is used when **one of several alternative paths** is to be followed depending on which transition condition becomes TRUE first (e.g., recipe selection based on product type).
- **AND Divergence (1.5 marks):** A **double horizontal line** placed on a vertical link. It is used when **two or more paths must be executed simultaneously** (e.g., starting the mixer motor and a process timer in parallel when the tank is full).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Style)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — SFC Questions**
> 1. **Do not forget the initial step** — A step sequence without a clearly marked initial (double-lined) step loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Always declare action qualifiers** — Writing `Motor = ON` is treated as incomplete; the qualifier (N, S, L, D, P, etc.) is mandatory for full marks.
> 3. **Transitions are Boolean expressions, not descriptions** — Writing "tank is full" is wrong; write `LT_A >= L_high` or `Level_High = 1`.
> 4. **One step = One stable state** — Do not pack two actions that are conceptually separate states into the same step.
> 5. **Branching lines must be drawn correctly** — Single line for OR, double line for AND. Examiners are strict on this distinction.

---

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample ESE Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a Sequential Function Chart as per IEC 61131-3. List the standard action qualifiers and explain any **four** of them with one example each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a complete SFC for an **automatic bottle filling station** with the following sequence: conveyor runs → bottle is detected at the filling nozzle → filling valve opens for a fixed time (use a timer) → cap is placed (simulate using a memory bit) → bottle is conveyed to the next station. Provide the I/O assignment and the action qualifiers. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Structure of SFC (3 marks):**
An SFC consists of:
- **Steps** (rectangles; double rectangle for initial step)
- **Transitions** (horizontal bars carrying Boolean conditions)
- **Action blocks** (rectangles connected to steps, containing the output logic)
- **Links** (vertical lines connecting the above)
- **Divergence / Convergence branches** (OR — single line; AND — double line)
- **Jumps** (for looping or skipping)

**Four Action Qualifiers (4 marks, 1 each):**

1. **N (Non-stored):** Action active as long as the step is active; de-energizes the moment the step becomes inactive.  
   *Example:* `Heater := S4 & N` — heater ON only while Step 4 is active.

2. **L (Time Limited):** Action is energized for a maximum time `T`, then automatically de-energizes even if the step remains active.  
   *Example:* `Fill_Valve := S2 & L(T#5s)` — filling valve ON for max 5 s.

3. **S (Stored / Set):** Once the step activates, the action latches ON and remains ON until explicitly Reset.  
   *Example:* `Alarm := S_alarm & S` — alarm latches until reset.

4. **P (Pulse):** Action executes for a single scan cycle when the step activates.  
   *Example:* `Counter_CV := S_count & P` — counter increments by one on entry to step.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**I/O Assignment Table (1 mark):**

| Symbol | Address | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `START_PB` | I0.0 | Start pushbutton (NO) |
| `STOP_PB` | I0.1 | Stop pushbutton (NC) |
| `BOTTLE_DET` | I0.2 | Bottle-at-nozzle proximity sensor |
| `CONVEYOR` | Q0.0 | Conveyor motor contactor |
| `FILL_VALVE` | Q0.1 | Filling solenoid valve |
| `CAP_SOL` | Q0.2 | Cap-placement solenoid |
| `NEXT_STAT` | Q0.3 | Indexing to next station |
| `T_FILL` | Timer | Filling-time timer (PT = 3 s) |
| `M_DONE` | M0.0 | Cap-placed memory bit |

**SFC Diagram (4 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    init([S0 Idle])
    s1[/S1 Conveyor ON/]
    s2[/S2 Bottle Detected/]
    s3[/S3 Filling Valve Open/]
    s4[/S4 Cap Placement/]
    s5[/S5 Index to Next/]

    tStart{START and not STOP}
    tDetect{BOTTLE_DET TRUE}
    tFill{T Fill Q TRUE 3s}
    tCap{M_DONE TRUE}
    tIndex{Indexing Timer Done}

    a1[/Conveyor N/]
    a2[/Fill Valve L 3s/]
    a3[/Cap Solenoid P/]
    a4[/Next Station P/]

    init --> tStart
    tStart --> s1
    s1 --- a1
    s1 --> tDetect
    tDetect --> s2
    s2 --> tFill
    tFill --> s3
    s3 --- a2
    s3 --> tCap
    tCap --> s4
    s4 --- a3
    s4 --> tIndex
    tIndex --> s5
    s5 --- a4
    s5 --> init
```

**Step-by-Step Action Listing (2 marks):**

- **S1:** `CONVEYOR := S1 & N`
- **S3:** `FILL_VALVE := S3 & L(T#3s)`; `T_FILL(IN := S3, PT := T#3s)`
- **S4:** `CAP_SOL := S4 & P`; `M_DONE := S4 & S`; `M_DONE := NOT S4 & R` after cap-placed feedback
- **S5:** `NEXT_STAT := S5 & P`

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample ESE Module 4 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of steps (initial vs. normal), transitions, and links used in SFC. Describe the **step-activity rule** of IEC 61131-3. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Develop a complete SFC for a **batch paint-mixing process** with the following recipe: Idle → Add pigment (Valve V1, until level LT1 = HIGH) → Add solvent (Valve V2, until level LT2 = HIGH) → Start disperser motor and run for 120 s → Stop disperser → Open drain valve V3 until level LT3 = LOW → Return to Idle. List the I/O and specify action qualifiers. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Types of Steps (2 marks):**
- **Initial Step:** A double-lined rectangle. There must be exactly one initial step per SFC network. It is active immediately after a cold or warm restart of the PLC. After a power-fail restart, SFC processing begins again from the initial step unless persistent (retained) step flags are configured.
- **Normal Step:** A single-lined rectangle. It is activated only when a transition immediately preceding it evaluates TRUE while its predecessor step is active. Each step has an associated boolean flag (e.g., `S3.X` in IEC 61131-3 nomenclature).

**Transitions (2 marks):**
A transition is a Boolean condition placed on a horizontal line that links two steps. A transition is evaluated continuously by the PLC scan engine. It may include I/O, step flags, timer/counter contacts, and comparison expressions. **A transition is passable only if all its predecessor steps are active** — this is the step-activity rule.

**Links (1 mark):**
Links are the vertical lines that connect steps, transitions, and action blocks. They can be of three types:
- **Normal link** (single line) — sequential flow
- **OR link** (single line with tick) — alternative branches
- **AND link** (double line) — parallel branches

**Step-Activity Rule (2 marks):**
Per IEC 61131-3, the **step-activity rule** states that a transition is cleared (passed) only when:
1. All steps directly connected to its top (predecessors) are active, **AND**
2. The Boolean expression associated with the transition evaluates to TRUE.

Once cleared, all predecessor steps are simultaneously deactivated, and all successor steps are activated in the **same scan cycle**. This guarantees deterministic, race-free transitions.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**I/O Assignment Table (1 mark):**

| Symbol | Address | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `START` | I0.0 | Start pushbutton |
| `LT1` | I0.1 | Pigment tank high-level switch |
| `LT2` | I0.2 | Solvent tank high-level switch |
| `LT3` | I0.3 | Mixer low-level switch |
| `V1` | Q0.0 | Pigment inlet valve |
| `V2` | Q0.1 | Solvent inlet valve |
| `M_DISP` | Q0.2 | Disperser motor contactor |
| `V3` | Q0.3 | Drain valve |
| `T_DISP` | Timer | Disperser timer (PT = 120 s) |

**SFC Diagram (4 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    idle([S0 Idle])
    s1[/S1 Add Pigment/]
    s2[/S2 Add Solvent/]
    s3[/S3 Disperse/]
    s4[/S4 Drain/]

    t1{START and not STOP}
    t2{LT1 TRUE}
    t3{LT2 TRUE}
    t4{T Disp Q TRUE 120s}
    t5{LT3 TRUE}

    a1[/V1 ON N/]
    a2[/V2 ON N/]
    a3[/M DISP ON N Timer Disp Start/]
    a4[/V3 ON N/]

    idle --> t1
    t1 --> s1
    s1 --- a1
    s1 --> t2
    t2 --> s2
    s2 --- a2
    s2 --> t3
    t3 --> s3
    s3 --- a3
    s3 --> t4
    t4 --> s4
    s4 --- a4
    s4 --> t5
    t5 --> idle
```

**Action Qualifier Listing (2 marks):**

- **S1:** `V1 := S1 & N` (pigment valve ON while step active)
- **S2:** `V2 := S2 & N` (solvent valve ON while step active)
- **S3:** `M_DISP := S3 & N`; `T_DISP(IN := S3, PT := T#120s)` (disperser runs, timer counts)
- **S4:** `V3 := S4 & N` (drain valve ON while step active)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **SFC is a graphical, state-based PLC language** standardized under **IEC 61131-3, Clause 6**.
- [x] Three core elements: **Steps**, **Transitions**, **Actions**.
- [x] The **initial step is double-lined**; there must be exactly one per SFC.
- [x] A **transition is passable only if its predecessor step is active AND the Boolean expression is TRUE** (step-activity rule).
- [x] **Action qualifiers (N, S, R, L, D, P, P1, P0, SD, DS, SL)** determine how an action behaves with respect to step activation time — always specify them in KTU answers.
- [x] **OR divergence / convergence** uses a **single line** (alternative branches).
- [x] **AND divergence / convergence** uses a **double line** (parallel paths).
- [x] SFC is **best suited for sequential batch processes** with clear recipes — common in pharma, food, paint, chemical, and water-treatment industries.
- [x] The **step activation Boolean equation** in a sequential SFC is:

$$
S_i(t) = \big[ S_{i-1}(t-1) \cdot T_{i-1}(t) \cdot \overline{T_i(t)} \big] \;\lor\; \big[ S_i(t-1) \cdot \overline{T_i(t)} \big]
$$

- [x] Safety interlocks should be implemented as **override transitions** parallel to the SFC for fail-safe behavior.
- [x] SFC programs are **easier to modify, visualize, and validate** than equivalent ladder logic for complex recipes.
- [x] Always provide the **I/O assignment table** before drawing the SFC in KTU exam answers.
- [x] Always **state the qualifier** next to every action (e.g., `Heater := S4 & L(T#600s)`).
- [x] Always include the **initial step** and a clearly marked **end step** if the batch terminates.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog Control Using PLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT831 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analog Control Using PLC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Analog Control Using PLC** refers to the process of continuously reading, processing, and generating real-valued (non-binary) electrical signals through a Programmable Logic Controller to regulate physical process variables such as temperature, pressure, flow, level, and motor speed. Unlike discrete (digital) control which deals with ON/OFF logic states, analog control operates over a continuous signal range — typically **0 V to 10 V (voltage)** or **4 mA to 20 mA (current)** — and requires dedicated **Analog Input (AI)** and **Analog Output (AO) modules** interfacing with the central PLC CPU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Analog control forms the bridge between the binary world of the PLC logic solver and the infinite-resolution continuous world of physical processes. The standard industrial signal is the **4–20 mA current loop**, valued for its noise immunity, live-zero fault detection, and long-distance transmission capability.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **car's cruise control system**:
- The driver sets a desired speed (e.g., 80 km/h) — this is the **Setpoint (SP)**.
- The dashboard speedometer reads the actual speed — this is the **Process Variable (PV)**.
- The accelerator pedal is pressed lightly or heavily based on the difference — this is the **Controller Output (CV)**.
- A PLC doing analog control behaves **exactly the same way**: it continuously reads the PV from a sensor, compares it to the SP, and smoothly adjusts the output to an actuator (valve, VFD, heater) to maintain the desired condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Definition:** An analog PLC module is essentially a **digital-to-analog and analog-to-digital translator** — it converts the real world's continuous physical quantities into binary numbers the CPU can crunch, and converts the CPU's digital decisions back into smooth, continuously variable signals for the actuators.

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| Voltage Signal Range | **0 V to +10 V DC** (also ±10 V bipolar) |
| Current Signal Range | **4 mA to 20 mA DC** |
| Live Zero | **4 mA** (any current < 4 mA = open loop / sensor fault) |
| Dead Zero (rare) | **0 mA** (no fault detection) |
| Typical Resolution | **12-bit (4096 steps), 14-bit, or 16-bit** |
| Scan Time | **1 ms to 20 ms** per analog channel |
| Common Sensor Outputs | RTD, Thermocouple (mV), 4–20 mA, 0–10 V, 0–30 psi |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of an Analog PLC System

The signal flow in an analog PLC control loop follows a strict sequence:

1. **Transduction (Sensor Stage):** A physical variable (e.g., temperature) is sensed by a transducer (e.g., RTD, thermocouple) and converted into a standardized electrical signal (e.g., 4–20 mA).
2. **Signal Conditioning:** The raw signal is filtered, amplified, linearized, and isolated before reaching the analog input module.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):** The AI module samples the continuous signal and quantizes it into a binary integer (e.g., 0–4095 for a 12-bit ADC).
4. **Scaling (Engineering Unit Conversion):** The raw integer count is mathematically mapped to its real engineering value (e.g., 0–4095 → 0–150 °C) using linear scaling equations.
5. **Control Algorithm Execution:** The CPU runs the user program — typically a **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative)** block — to compute the required corrective output.
6. **Digital-to-Analog Conversion (DAC):** The AO module converts the controller's digital output integer into a continuous voltage or current signal.
7. **Actuation:** The analog signal drives a final control element (control valve, variable frequency drive, SCR power controller) to modulate the process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the 4–20 mA Standard?**
> - **Current is immune to voltage drops** over long cable runs.
> - **Live-zero (4 mA ≠ 0)** allows the PLC to distinguish a true zero reading from a broken wire (which would read 0 mA).
> - **4 mA provides power** to energize the transmitter's internal circuitry (loop-powered devices).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Note:** All vertical bars for absolute value are rendered using `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ADC Resolution** | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{FS Range}}{2^n}$ | $n$ = number of bits of the ADC. Determines the smallest detectable signal change. |
| 2 | **Raw Count to Engineering Value (Scaling Up)** | $E = \left( \dfrac{\text{Raw} - \text{Raw}_{\min}}{\text{Raw}_{\max} - \text{Raw}_{\min}} \right) \times (E_{\max} - E_{\min}) + E_{\min}$ | Converts the ADC's integer output into a real engineering unit (°C, kPa, etc.). |
| 3 | **Engineering Value to Raw Count (Scaling Down)** | $\text{Raw} = \left( \dfrac{E - E_{\min}}{E_{\max} - E_{\min}} \right) \times (\text{Raw}_{\max} - \text{Raw}_{\min}) + \text{Raw}_{\min}$ | Used to command an analog output. |
| 4 | **Current-Loop to Engineering Value** | $E = \left( \dfrac{I - 4 \text{ mA}}{16 \text{ mA}} \right) \times (E_{\max} - E_{\min}) + E_{\min}$ | The live-zero standard for 4–20 mA loops. |
| 5 | **Voltage-Loop to Engineering Value** | $E = \left( \dfrac{V - 0 \text{ V}}{10 \text{ V}} \right) \times (E_{\max} - E_{\min}) + E_{\min}$ | The 0–10 V standard. |
| 6 | **PID Controller Output (Position Form)** | $CV(t) = K_c e(t) + \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_i} \int e(t)\,dt + K_c \tau_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $K_c$ = proportional gain, $\tau_i$ = integral time, $\tau_d$ = derivative time, $e(t) = SP - PV$. |
| 7 | **PID Velocity (Incremental) Form** | $\Delta CV = K_c [ (e_n - e_{n-1}) + \dfrac{T}{\tau_i} e_n + \dfrac{\tau_d}{T} (e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2}) ]$ | $T$ = sample period. Avoids output bump on setpoint change. |
| 8 | **Process Variable Error** | $e(t) = SP - PV$ | The deviation between the desired and measured value. |
| 9 | **Output Clamping** | $0\% \le CV \le 100\%$ | Physical actuator limits. |
| 10 | **Percent Scale** | $\% \text{ Scale} = \dfrac{E - E_{\min}}{E_{\max} - E_{\min}} \times 100$ | Normalized 0–100% representation, common in HMI displays. |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Process Industries:** Continuous temperature control of chemical reactors using PID loops implemented in PLC analog output modules driving SCR-fired heaters.
- **Manufacturing:** Speed regulation of conveyors and mixers via analog commands sent to Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs).
- **Water Treatment:** Level control in tanks using 4–20 mA level transmitters and proportional control valves.
- **Energy:** Pressure control in compressor stations with cascaded PID loops (master flow controller → slave pressure controller).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Resolution of an Analog Input Module

**Problem Statement:** A 12-bit analog input module is configured for the standard 0–10 V voltage range. Determine (a) the resolution in volts per least-significant bit (LSB), and (b) the raw integer count when the input voltage is 6.4 V.

**Given Data:**
- Number of bits, $n = 12$
- Full-scale range, $V_{FS} = 10 \text{ V} - 0 \text{ V} = 10 \text{ V}$
- Input voltage, $V_{in} = 6.4 \text{ V}$

**Step 1 — Total Number of Discrete Steps**

A binary ADC of $n$ bits can represent $2^n$ distinct quantization levels.

$$
N_{steps} = 2^{12} = 4096
$$

**Step 2 — Resolution per LSB (Volts per Count)**

The resolution is the full-scale span divided by the total number of steps. For a unipolar range starting at 0, the formula is:

$$
\text{Resolution}_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\text{Resolution}_{LSB} = \dfrac{10 \text{ V}}{4096} \approx 0.00244 \text{ V/count} = 2.44 \text{ mV/count}
$$

**Step 3 — Raw Integer Count for $V_{in} = 6.4$ V**

The integer count is the input voltage divided by the per-bit resolution:

$$
\text{Raw Count} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{\text{Resolution}_{LSB}} = \dfrac{6.4 \text{ V}}{0.00244 \text{ V/count}}
$$

Performing the division:

$$
\text{Raw Count} = 2621.44 \approx 2621
$$

Since the ADC is an integer-quantizing device, the actual closest representation is **2621 counts**, corresponding to a measured voltage of:

$$
V_{measured} = 2621 \times 0.00244 \text{ V} = 6.395 \text{ V}
$$

**Quantization Error** (worst-case): $\pm \dfrac{1}{2} \text{ LSB} = \pm 1.22 \text{ mV}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 12-bit ADC gives 4096 levels — sufficient for most industrial applications. However, for ultra-precise weighing or laboratory instrumentation, 16-bit (65,536 levels) modules are used, offering a resolution of $10\,\text{V} \div 65536 \approx 0.15 \text{ mV/count}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: 4–20 mA Current-Loop Scaling to Engineering Units

**Problem Statement:** A pressure transmitter with a 4–20 mA output is connected to a PLC analog input. The transmitter is calibrated to measure 0–10 bar. The PLC reads a raw count of 20480 from a 16-bit module (range 0–65535 corresponding to 0–20 mA). What is the actual measured pressure?

**Given Data:**
- Current loop standard: $I_{\min} = 4 \text{ mA}$, $I_{\max} = 20 \text{ mA}$
- Pressure range: $E_{\min} = 0 \text{ bar}$, $E_{\max} = 10 \text{ bar}$
- Raw count: $\text{Raw} = 20480$ (out of 16-bit max $65535$)

**Step 1 — Map the Raw Count to the Live-Zero Range**

A 16-bit module reports 0 at 0 mA and 65535 at 20 mA (unipolar). The raw count corresponding to 4 mA is:

$$
\text{Raw}_{4mA} = \dfrac{4}{20} \times 65535 = 13107
$$

The raw count corresponding to 20 mA is:

$$
\text{Raw}_{20mA} = 65535
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Linear Scaling Equation**

$$
E = \left( \dfrac{\text{Raw} - \text{Raw}_{4mA}}{\text{Raw}_{20mA} - \text{Raw}_{4mA}} \right) \times (E_{\max} - E_{\min}) + E_{\min}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Values**

$$
E = \left( \dfrac{20480 - 13107}{65535 - 13107} \right) \times (10 - 0) + 0
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the Numerator and Denominator**

$$
E = \left( \dfrac{7373}{52428} \right) \times 10
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Final Result**

$$
E = 0.14064 \times 10 = 1.4064 \text{ bar}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Check:** The raw count 20480 corresponds to $\dfrac{20480}{65535} \times 20 \text{ mA} = 6.25 \text{ mA}$. The live-zero range $4 \to 20 \text{ mA}$ is $16 \text{ mA}$ wide; $6.25 - 4 = 2.25 \text{ mA}$ above live-zero, i.e., $\dfrac{2.25}{16} \times 10 \text{ bar} = 1.406 \text{ bar}$. ✅ Confirmed.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Control Strategy

| Process Type | Recommended Strategy | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Large dead-time** (e.g., long pipelines) | **PI with feed-forward** | Pure PID causes oscillation; integral action corrects steady-state error. |
| **Fast-response, no steady-state error tolerance** | **PID (full form)** | Derivative action anticipates errors; integral eliminates offset. |
| **On/Off control acceptable** (e.g., household thermostat) | **Two-position (hysteresis) control** | Cheap; no analog I/O strictly needed. |
| **Continuous flow / pressure regulation** | **PI with cascade** | Outer loop (master) sets setpoint of inner loop (slave) for faster disturbance rejection. |
| **Batch process with frequent setpoint changes** | **Velocity-form PID** | Prevents output "bump" on setpoint change. |
| **Non-linear valve characteristics** | **PID with characterized output (e.g., equal-percentage)** | Compensates for installed valve non-linearity. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Analog Control Loop using PLC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Process Variable: Temperature, Pressure, Flow, Level] --> B[Sensor / Transducer: RTD, Thermocouple, Pressure TX]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioner: Filter, Amplify, Linearize, Isolate]
    C --> D[Analog Input Module AI: 4-20 mA or 0-10 V]
    D --> E[ADC: Analog to Digital Converter]
    E --> F[PLC CPU: Engineering Unit Scaling and PID Control Algorithm]
    F --> G[DAC: Digital to Analog Converter]
    G --> H[Analog Output Module AO: 4-20 mA or 0-10 V]
    H --> I[Final Control Element: Control Valve, VFD, SCR Heater]
    I --> J[Actuator modulates the Process]
    J --> A
    K[Operator HMI: Setpoint SP Entry] --> F
    F --> L[Feedback to HMI: Process Value PV and Controller Output CV]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — PID Controller Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> SUM[ Summing Junction: e = SP - PV ]
    PV[Process Variable PV from ADC] --> SUM
    SUM --> P[Proportional Term: Kc x e]
    SUM --> I[Integral Term: Kc over ti times integral of e dt]
    SUM --> D[Derivative Term: Kc x td x de dt]
    P --> ADD[ Summation Node: CV = P + I + D ]
    I --> ADD
    D --> ADD
    ADD --> CLAMP[Output Clamp: 0 percent to 100 percent]
    CLAMP --> OUT[Controller Output CV to DAC]
    OUT --> AO[Analog Output Module]
    AO --> ACT[Actuator: Valve or VFD]
    ACT --> PROC[Process]
    PROC --> TRANS[Sensor]
    TRANS --> ADC[Analog Input Module]
    ADC --> PV
```

### 4.3 Module Architecture — Typical PLC Analog I/O Module

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph F[Field Wiring Side]
        A1[Input 1: 4-20 mA from Field]
        A2[Input 2: 0-10 V from Field]
        A3[Output 1: 4-20 mA to Valve]
    end
    subgraph M[Analog Module Front End]
        B1[Multiplexer: Select Channel]
        B2[Programmable Gain Amplifier PGA]
        B3[Anti-aliasing Low-pass Filter]
    end
    subgraph C[Conversion Stage]
        C1[12 / 14 / 16 bit ADC]
        C2[16 bit DAC]
    end
    subgraph CPU[PLC CPU Backplane]
        D1[Image Table: Integer Registers IW0, IW2, ...]
        D2[PID Instruction Block]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> C1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> C2 --> A3
```

### 4.4 Comparison: Voltage Loop vs Current Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph V[Voltage Loop 0 to 10 V]
        V1[Sensor] -->|Wire resistance causes drop| V2[V measured at PLC lower than actual]
        V2 --> V3[No fault detection if wire breaks]
    end
    subgraph I[Current Loop 4 to 20 mA]
        I1[Sensor] -->|Current same regardless of wire length| I2[I measured at PLC equals actual]
        I2 --> I3[Live zero detection: 0 mA means broken wire]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. List any three advantages of the 4–20 mA current loop over the 0–10 V voltage signal in industrial analog instrumentation.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Noise Immunity:** Current signals are immune to induced electromagnetic noise and voltage drops across long cable runs because the current is the same at both ends of the loop.
2. **Live-Zero Fault Detection:** A true zero on the scale is represented by 4 mA, not 0 mA. Hence, if the PLC receives 0 mA, it can immediately flag a broken wire or transmitter failure.
3. **Loop-Powered Capability:** The 4 mA minimum provides sufficient power to energize the transmitter's internal circuitry, allowing two-wire loop-powered field devices (no separate power supply required at the field).
4. **Long-Distance Accuracy:** Voltage signals suffer $I \cdot R$ drops over kilometres of cable; current loops do not, ensuring accuracy over long distances.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. A 14-bit analog input module is used to measure a signal in the range 0–10 V. Calculate the resolution of the module in volts per LSB.**

**Model Answer:**

Given: $n = 14$ bits, $V_{FS} = 10 \text{ V}$.

$$
\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{10 \text{ V}}{2^{14}} = \dfrac{10}{16384} \approx 0.000610 \text{ V/count} = 0.61 \text{ mV/count}
$$

**Resolution = 0.61 mV per LSB.**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question — Module 4 Choice A]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an **analog control loop using a PLC**. Identify the role of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **temperature transmitter** has a range of 0–200 °C and outputs a **4–20 mA** signal. The signal is fed to a **12-bit** PLC analog input module scaled to 0–20 mA. The PLC reads a raw count of **3072**. Calculate:
- (i) The actual temperature being measured. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) The percent scale of the measurement. **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) The current flowing in the loop. **(2 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The analog control loop consists of the following blocks in sequence:

1. **Process:** The physical system whose variable is to be controlled (e.g., furnace temperature).
2. **Sensor/Transmitter:** Converts the physical variable into a standardized electrical signal (e.g., RTD → 4–20 mA).
3. **Signal Conditioner:** Filters noise, amplifies weak signals (e.g., thermocouple mV), and linearizes non-linear sensor outputs.
4. **Analog Input Module + ADC:** Samples and quantizes the continuous signal into a digital integer (e.g., 0–4095 for 12-bit).
5. **PLC CPU — Scaling and Control Algorithm:** Performs engineering unit conversion and runs the PID algorithm.
6. **DAC + Analog Output Module:** Converts the digital output back to a 4–20 mA or 0–10 V signal.
7. **Final Control Element (Actuator):** Control valve, VFD, SCR, or damper that physically modulates the process.
8. **HMI:** Operator interface for setpoint entry, PV display, and CV monitoring.

**Role of each block:** The loop forms a **negative feedback system** — the measured PV is compared to the SP, the error drives the controller, which commands the actuator, which in turn adjusts the process, completing the loop.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- Temperature range: $E_{\min} = 0 \text{ °C}$, $E_{\max} = 200 \text{ °C}$
- Current loop: $I_{\min} = 4 \text{ mA}$, $I_{\max} = 20 \text{ mA}$
- ADC: 12-bit, scaled 0–20 mA → $0 \to 4095$ counts
- Raw count: $\text{Raw} = 3072$

**(i) Actual Temperature:**

Raw count at 4 mA:

$$
\text{Raw}_{4mA} = \dfrac{4}{20} \times 4095 = 819
$$

Raw count at 20 mA:

$$
\text{Raw}_{20mA} = 4095
$$

Applying the scaling equation:

$$
E = \left( \dfrac{3072 - 819}{4095 - 819} \right) \times (200 - 0) + 0
$$

$$
E = \left( \dfrac{2253}{3276} \right) \times 200
$$

$$
E = 0.6878 \times 200 = 137.56 \text{ °C}
$$

**Actual Temperature ≈ 137.56 °C**

**(ii) Percent Scale:**

$$
\% \text{Scale} = \dfrac{137.56 - 0}{200 - 0} \times 100 = 68.78\%
$$

**(iii) Loop Current:**

$$
I = \left( \dfrac{3072}{4095} \right) \times 20 \text{ mA} = 15.005 \text{ mA} \approx 15.0 \text{ mA}
$$

**Loop Current ≈ 15.0 mA**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting the live-zero:** Students often assume 0 mA = 0 °C, leading to a wrong answer. **Always subtract the 4 mA offset.**
> - **Off-by-one error in raw count range:** 12-bit = $2^{12} = 4096$ levels, but the maximum count is $4095$ (zero-indexed). Use **4095**, not 4096.
> - **Mixing up current-loop and voltage-loop formulas:** Ensure the live-zero is correctly handled in the numerator's lower bound.
> - **Forgetting units in the final answer:** Always state the unit (°C, mA, %) explicitly to score full marks.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question — Module 4 Choice B]**

**Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the **PID control algorithm** as implemented in a PLC. Write the **position form** of the PID equation and define each term. Discuss the effect of increasing the **proportional gain ($K_c$)**, **integral time ($\tau_i$)**, and **derivative time ($\tau_d$)** on the closed-loop response. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PLC is to control a **flow control valve** through a 4–20 mA analog output. The valve is fully closed at 4 mA and fully open at 20 mA. If the PID controller outputs a value of **45%**, calculate:
- (i) The analog output current. **(2 Marks)**
- (ii) The raw count that must be written to a 12-bit AO module scaled 0–20 mA. **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) Comment on the valve position. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

The **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative)** controller is the workhorse of continuous industrial control. The **position form** of the PID equation is:

$$
CV(t) = K_c \, e(t) + \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_i} \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_c \, \tau_d \, \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

**Definitions:**

- $CV(t)$ — Controller Output (position of the final control element, 0–100%).
- $e(t)$ — Error signal: $e(t) = SP - PV$ (deviation from setpoint).
- $K_c$ — Proportional gain (dimensionless, but defines controller sensitivity).
- $\tau_i$ — Integral time (seconds, also called "reset time").
- $\tau_d$ — Derivative time (seconds, also called "rate time").
- $t$ — Time (seconds).

**Effects of Tuning Parameters:**

| Parameter | Increase → | Decrease → |
|---|---|---|
| **$K_c$ (Proportional gain)** | Faster response, smaller steady-state error, but more overshoot and possible oscillation. | Slower response, larger steady-state error, more stable. |
| **$\tau_i$ (Integral time)** | Slower integral action, less aggressive correction of offset, more stable. | Faster integral action, quicker elimination of offset, but risk of oscillation and integrator windup. |
| **$\tau_d$ (Derivative time)** | More damping, better anticipation of error, less overshoot, but amplifies measurement noise. | Less damping, more oscillatory response, but lower noise sensitivity. |

**Practical Notes:**
- A **PI controller** is most common in industry; derivative action is sensitive to noise and is often omitted.
- **Derivative-on-PV** is preferred over derivative-on-error to avoid "derivative kick" on setpoint change.
- **Anti-windup** mechanisms (clamping or back-calculation) are mandatory when the output saturates.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- Valve: fully closed at 4 mA, fully open at 20 mA.
- PID output (normalized): $CV = 45\%$
- AO module: 12-bit, scaled 0–20 mA → $0 \to 4095$ counts.

**(i) Analog Output Current:**

$$
I_{out} = I_{\min} + \left( \dfrac{CV\%}{100\%} \right) \times (I_{\max} - I_{\min})
$$

$$
I_{out} = 4 \text{ mA} + 0.45 \times (20 - 4) \text{ mA} = 4 + 0.45 \times 16 = 4 + 7.2
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{out} = 11.2 \text{ mA}}
$$

**(ii) Raw Count for 12-bit AO Module:**

The 12-bit AO module writes 0 at 0 mA and 4095 at 20 mA. To find the count for 11.2 mA:

$$
\text{Raw} = \left( \dfrac{11.2 \text{ mA}}{20 \text{ mA}} \right) \times 4095 = 0.56 \times 4095 = 2293.2
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Raw Count} \approx 2293}
$$

**(iii) Valve Position Comment:**

The valve is at **45% open** — it is in the **lower half** of its travel, near the fully closed position. Since 4 mA = closed and 20 mA = open, the valve is partially open, allowing reduced but non-zero flow. The actuator is operating **well within its linear range**, providing smooth throttling control suitable for proportional flow regulation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting to clamp $CV$ to 0–100%** before scaling: Some students apply 45% directly as "0.45 A" — a unit and scaling error.
> - **Confusing position form vs velocity form:** In the position form, $CV$ is the absolute output; in velocity form, $\Delta CV$ is the *change* in output.
> - **Not explaining tuning trade-offs:** Marks are specifically awarded for linking each parameter to overshoot, stability, and steady-state error. A bare equation without the qualitative discussion loses 3–4 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Analog I/O modules** convert between continuous real-world signals and discrete digital integers using **ADC (input)** and **DAC (output)**.
- ✅ **Standard industrial signals:** **0–10 V** (short distances) and **4–20 mA** (long distances, fault-detectable).
- ✅ **Live-zero (4 mA)** enables wire-break detection; reading **0 mA = sensor/loop fault**.
- ✅ **Resolution formula:** $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{FS Range}}{2^n}$; higher bits = finer resolution.
- ✅ **12-bit ADC** → 4095 counts max (zero-indexed); **16-bit ADC** → 65535 counts max.
- ✅ **Scaling equation** is the most important formula on the exam — memorize it in both directions.
- ✅ **PID position form** has three terms: P (present), I (past), D (future prediction). Each affects response uniquely.
- ✅ **Tuning trade-off:** Higher $K_c$ → faster but more oscillatory; lower $\tau_i$ → faster integral but risk of windup; higher $\tau_d$ → more damping but noise-sensitive.
- ✅ **Derivative-on-PV** is preferred over derivative-on-error to avoid setpoint "derivative kick."
- ✅ **Anti-windup** is essential when the controller output saturates (beyond 0% or 100%).
- ✅ **Current loops** are immune to voltage drops; **voltage loops** are not — use current for long cable runs.
- ✅ **Engineering units** (°C, kPa, L/min) are what operators see; raw counts are internal to the PLC.
- ✅ **Practical application patterns:** Temperature → PID → SCR/Heater; Flow → PI → Control Valve; Speed → PID → VFD.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLC Interface to SCADA/DCS using Communication Links (RS232, RS485) and Protocols (Modbus ASCII/RTU)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT831 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** PLC Interface to SCADA/DCS using Communication Links (RS232, RS485) and Protocols (Modbus ASCII/RTU)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Big Picture: Why Do We Need Communication Interfaces?

In a modern industrial plant, a single **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** sitting on the shop floor cannot operate in isolation. It must report data to a **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA)** workstation in the control room, and it must coexist with neighbouring controllers inside a **Distributed Control System (DCS)**. The "language" they use to talk to each other is built on three layers:

1. The **Physical Layer** — the actual wires and electrical signals (RS232 or RS485).
2. The **Data Link Layer** — how bits are framed, addressed, and error-checked (Modbus ASCII or Modbus RTU).
3. The **Application Layer** — what the messages *mean* (read coil, write register, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 explicitly tests the ability of students to *differentiate* RS232 vs RS485, *select* a suitable protocol for a given plant scenario, and *calculate* error-checking codes (LRC and CRC-16) for Modbus frames.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Board-Examiner Standard)

**Programmable Logic Controller (PLC):** A ruggedized, solid-state industrial computer designed to read input signals from sensors, execute user-programmed logic (ladder, FBD, ST), and produce output commands to actuators in real time under harsh electrical, thermal, and mechanical environments.

**SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition):** A centralized software-hardware system that polls remote field devices (PLCs, RTUs, sensors) to acquire process variables, displays them on HMI screens, logs them, and sends supervisory setpoints back. SCADA is *monitoring-oriented* and historically used in geographically spread assets (pipelines, power grids).

**DCS (Distributed Control System):** An integrated network of controllers (and I/O subsystems) physically distributed across the plant, sharing a common process database and engineering workstation. DCS is *control-oriented* and designed for tightly coupled, continuous processes (refineries, petrochemical).

**RS232 (Recommended Standard 232):** A single-ended, point-to-point, voltage-based serial communication standard defined by the **Electronic Industries Association (EIA)** in 1969, with voltage levels of **±3 V to ±25 V** representing logic 1 (mark) and logic 0 (space) respectively.

**RS485 (Recommended Standard 485):** A differential, multi-drop, half-duplex (or full-duplex with 4 wires) serial communication standard defined by **TIA/EIA-485-A**, capable of connecting up to **32 unit loads (256 nodes with modern transceivers)** over a maximum cable length of **1200 m (4000 ft)**.

**Modbus:** An open, royalty-free, master-slave (client-server) application-layer protocol originally published by **Modicon (now Schneider Electric)** in **1979** for communicating with PLCs. It exists in two serial variants — **Modbus ASCII** and **Modbus RTU** — differing only in framing and error checking.

---

## 1.3 The Real-World Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Office Post System**
>
> Imagine a factory as a huge office building. The **PLC** is the *employee* on the shop floor (Factory Floor) who works with raw materials. The **SCADA** system is the *manager's cabin* on the 5th floor, and the **DCS** is the *networked team of managers* sharing the same database.
>
> - **RS232** is like a *landline telephone* directly connecting two specific people — only one call at a time, short distance, very simple.
> - **RS485** is like an *intercom / PA system* — many people can listen, only one microphone talks at a time, but the announcement can travel across a long hallway.
> - **Modbus** is the *language* of the announcement: a strict format (who is calling, what is being asked, the question, and a signature proving it wasn't garbled).
> - **Modbus ASCII** = the announcement is spelled out letter-by-letter in human-readable English (slow but easy to debug on a screen).
> - **Modbus RTU** = the announcement is sent as a compact binary code (very fast, but you need a special device to "read" it).

---

## 1.4 Distinguishing SCADA vs DCS at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Is SCADA a subset of DCS?"* The technically precise answer is **No** — they are parallel architectures that have *converged* with modern technology. The historical distinction lies in *geography* (SCADA = wide-area, DCS = plant-wide) and *control philosophy* (SCADA = supervisory, DCS = regulatory and supervisory both).

| Feature | SCADA | DCS |
|---|---|---|
| Geographic focus | Wide-area, geographically dispersed | Local, single plant |
| Primary function | Monitoring & supervisory control | Closed-loop regulatory control + monitoring |
| Redundancy philosophy | Backup master, radio fallback | Hot-standby controllers, redundant I/O buses |
| Database | Polled, event-driven | Continuously updated, shared process database |
| Typical industry | Power, water, pipeline, rail | Refinery, chemical, pharma, paper |
| Latency tolerance | Seconds to minutes | 100 ms – 1 s |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 RS232 — The Point-to-Point Veteran

### 2.1.1 Electrical Characteristics

- **Logic 1 (Mark):** Voltage between **−3 V to −25 V** (typically −12 V)
- **Logic 0 (Space):** Voltage between **+3 V to +25 V** (typically +12 V)
- **Single-ended signalling:** The signal is referenced to a common ground.
- **Maximum cable length:** **15 m (50 ft)** at the maximum specified bit rate of **20 kbps**. (In practice, lower baud rates can push it to ~30 m, but KTU boards expect the official **15 m**.)
- **Standard connectors:** **DB-9 (DE-9)** or **DB-25**.
- **Modes of operation:** Simplex, half-duplex, or full-duplex (separate TX and RX lines).

### 2.1.2 The DB-9 (DE-9) Pinout Every KTU Student Must Memorise

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"List the function of the handshaking pins in RS232."* Memorise the table below.

| Pin | Name | Direction (DTE → DCE) | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DCD (Data Carrier Detect) | IN | Indicates carrier detected |
| 2 | RXD (Received Data) | IN | Data from DCE to DTE |
| 3 | TXD (Transmitted Data) | OUT | Data from DTE to DCE |
| 4 | DTR (Data Terminal Ready) | OUT | DTE is ready |
| 5 | GND (Signal Ground) | — | Common reference |
| 6 | DSR (Data Set Ready) | IN | DCE is ready |
| 7 | RTS (Request To Send) | OUT | DTE wants to transmit |
| 8 | CTS (Clear To Send) | IN | DCE grants permission |
| 9 | RI (Ring Indicator) | IN | Incoming call signal |

The "Why" — Handshaking pins prevent **data overrun** by ensuring both ends agree *when* bytes may be sent. Without them, a fast transmitter could overwhelm a slow receiver, corrupting frames.

### 2.1.3 Limitations of RS232

- Only **1 driver and 1 receiver** on the bus → no multi-drop.
- Susceptible to **ground loops** and **common-mode noise** because of single-ended signalling.
- Short distance makes it impractical for plant-wide deployment.

---

## 2.2 RS485 — The Industrial Workhorse

### 2.2.1 Electrical Characteristics

- **Differential signalling:** Information is carried by the *voltage difference* between two wires (A and B, also called D+ and D−).
- **Logic state:** Voltage difference of **≥ +200 mV** = Mark (1); **≤ −200 mV** = Space (0).
- **Maximum cable length:** **1200 m (4000 ft)** at **100 kbps**. The product of *baud rate × distance* is roughly constant at **10⁸**.
- **Maximum nodes:** **32 standard unit loads** per segment; with modern transceivers, up to **256** are possible.
- **Topology:** Daisy-chain only — *not* star, *not* ring. Stubs must be shorter than one-tenth of the driver's rise time.
- **Termination:** **120 Ω** resistor at each *physical* end of the cable to match its characteristic impedance and suppress reflections.

### 2.2.2 Why Differential Signalling Wins in the Plant

> [!NOTE]
> **The Subway Sandwich Analogy**
> Single-ended signalling (RS232) is like sending a *single truck* with goods. If a pothole shakes the truck, some goods fall out. Differential signalling (RS485) is like sending *two identical trucks side by side*. The receiver doesn't care about the road's bumps — it only looks at the *difference* between the two trucks. Both get shaken, but their difference is preserved. This is called **Common-Mode Rejection**.

### 2.2.3 RS232 vs RS485 — KTU High-Yield Comparison

| Parameter | RS232 | RS485 |
|---|---|---|
| Signalling | Single-ended (unbalanced) | Differential (balanced) |
| Voltage levels | ±3 V to ±25 V | ±1.5 V to ±6 V (driver), ±200 mV (receiver sensitivity) |
| Max distance | 15 m | 1200 m |
| Max devices | 2 (1 driver, 1 receiver) | 32 unit loads (up to 256) |
| Topology | Point-to-point | Multi-drop (daisy-chain) |
| Duplex | Full-duplex (separate TX/RX) | Half-duplex (2-wire) or full-duplex (4-wire) |
| Noise immunity | Low | High (CMRR > 60 dB typical) |
| Connector | DB-9, DB-25 | Screw terminals, RJ-45 |
| Max baud rate | 20 kbps (spec); 115 kbps typical | 10 Mbps (short distance); 10 Mbps at < 6 m, 100 kbps at 1200 m |

---

## 2.3 The Modbus Protocol Family

### 2.3.1 Modbus — The Master-Slave (Client-Server) Model

- **Master (Client):** Initiates all transactions. There is **exactly one master** per bus.
- **Slave (Server):** Never speaks unless spoken to. Up to **247 slaves** addressable (addresses **1–247**; 0 = broadcast).
- A typical transaction: Master sends a *Request PDU*; slave replies with a *Response PDU*; or the master may broadcast a *command* (no reply expected).

### 2.3.2 Modbus ASCII vs Modbus RTU — The Core Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners love asking: *"Compare Modbus ASCII and Modbus RTU. When would you choose one over the other?"* The table below is your complete answer template.

| Parameter | Modbus ASCII | Modbus RTU |
|---|---|---|
| Encoding | ASCII hex characters (printable) | Binary 8-bit bytes |
| Start marker | `:` (colon, ASCII 0x3A) | **3.5 character silence** (≥ 28 bits at 9600) |
| End marker | **CR + LF** (0x0D, 0x0A) | **3.5 character silence** |
| Error check | **LRC (Longitudinal Redundancy Check)** — 8-bit, 2 hex characters | **CRC-16 (Cyclic Redundancy Check)** — 16-bit, 2 bytes |
| Byte format | 7 bits, even parity, 1 stop (typical 7E1) | 8 bits, no parity, 1 stop (typical 8N1) |
| Throughput | Lower (each byte → 2 ASCII chars) | Higher (compact binary) |
| Human readability | Yes — can be read on a terminal | No — needs protocol analyser |
| Typical use | Slow, diagnostic-friendly links | High-speed production links |
| Time-sensitivity | Tolerant of inter-character gaps (≤ 1 s) | **Strict** — gaps within a frame must be < 1.5 char times |

### 2.3.3 Modbus Frame Structure (PDU + Addressing)

A Modbus frame has the **Address byte**, the **Protocol Data Unit (PDU)**, and an **Error Check**.

| Field | Modbus ASCII | Modbus RTU |
|---|---|---|
| Start | 1 char `:` (0x3A) | None (silence) |
| Address | 2 hex chars | 1 byte |
| Function code | 2 hex chars | 1 byte |
| Data | 2n hex chars | n bytes |
| Error check | LRC: 2 hex chars | CRC-16: 2 bytes |
| End | CR LF | None (silence) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Function Codes — The Vocabulary of Modbus**
> The most frequently tested KTU function codes are:
> - **01** — Read Coils
> - **02** — Read Discrete Inputs
> - **03** — Read Holding Registers
> - **04** — Read Input Registers
> - **05** — Write Single Coil
> - **06** — Write Single Register
> - **15 (0x0F)** — Write Multiple Coils
> - **16 (0x10)** — Write Multiple Registers
>
> Exception responses use the same function code **+ 0x80**, with an exception code (1 = Illegal Function, 2 = Illegal Data Address, 3 = Illegal Data Value, etc.).

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Error Checks

Industrial environments are electrically *noisy* — VFDs, contactors, and lightning-induced surges constantly corrupt serial bits. Modbus therefore attaches a mathematically derived *checksum* to every frame:

- **LRC (8-bit)** — fast, weak, only good for ASCII's slow speed.
- **CRC-16** — strong, polynomial-based, catches all single-bit and most multi-bit errors (Hamming distance = 4 for the standard polynomial 0xA001).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table — it is the single highest-frequency content in KTU Board exams for this topic.

| Quantity | Value / Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| RS232 max distance | **15 m** | metres |
| RS232 logic 1 voltage | **−3 V to −25 V** | volts (negative = 1) |
| RS485 max distance | **1200 m** | metres |
| RS485 max nodes (standard) | **32** | unit loads |
| RS485 termination | **120 Ω** | ohms (resistor at each end) |
| Modbus max slaves | **247** | addresses 1–247 |
| Modbus broadcast address | **0** | no reply |
| ASCII start delimiter | `:` = **0x3A** | ASCII |
| ASCII end delimiter | **CR LF** = 0x0D 0x0A | ASCII |
| RTU start/end gap | **3.5 character times** | at given baud |
| Modbus ASCII error check | **LRC (8-bit)** | 2 hex chars |
| Modbus RTU error check | **CRC-16 (polynomial 0xA001)** | 2 bytes |
| CRC-16 initial value | **0xFFFF** | per Modbus spec |
| LRC = | **−(Sum of all bytes mod 256)** | two's complement |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section contains the **only** mathematically examinable content for this topic: the construction of LRC and CRC-16 checksums. KTU 2024 boards routinely ask students to compute these for a given hex string. The worked examples below are exhaustive.

---

## 3.1 LRC Calculation for Modbus ASCII (Full Derivation)

### 3.1.1 The Underlying Math

Let the message (excluding the start colon and the LRC byte itself) consist of bytes $b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n$. The **8-bit Longitudinal Redundancy Check (LRC)** is defined as the two's complement of the sum of all bytes, taken modulo 256.

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i \pmod{256}
$$

$$
\text{LRC} = (-S) \pmod{256} = \overline{S} + 1 \pmod{256}
$$

That is, you **add all bytes, keep only the lowest 8 bits, then take the two's complement**.

### 3.1.2 Worked Example — "Read Holding Register from Slave 0x01, Register 0x0002"

The user-data field (the bytes *before* the LRC) for this request is: `01 03 00 02 00 01`.

**Step 1 — Add all bytes (decimal, then convert):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_1 &= 0x01 = 1 \\
b_2 &= 0x03 = 3 \\
b_3 &= 0x00 = 0 \\
b_4 &= 0x02 = 2 \\
b_5 &= 0x00 = 0 \\
b_6 &= 0x01 = 1 \\
S_{\text{decimal}} &= 1 + 3 + 0 + 2 + 0 + 1 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Reduce mod 256:**

$$
S = 7 \pmod{256} = 7
$$

**Step 3 — Take two's complement of 7:**

The two's complement of an 8-bit number $x$ is $(256 - x) \pmod{256}$.

$$
\text{LRC} = (256 - 7) \pmod{256} = 249
$$

**Step 4 — Convert 249 to hex:** 249 in hex is **0xF9**.

**Step 5 — Build the full ASCII frame:**

The frame becomes `: 01 03 00 02 00 01 F9 CR LF` where every byte is replaced by its two-character ASCII hex representation:

$$
\boxed{\texttt{:010300020001F9\r\n}}
$$

**Step 6 — Receiver Verification (why this works):**

On the receiving side, the slave (or master) sums **all bytes including the LRC**. If the result modulo 256 is zero, the frame is accepted.

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} b_i \pmod{256} = 7 + 249 = 256 \equiv 0 \pmod{256} \;\;\checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This elegant property is the key insight KTU examiners want students to articulate: **Adding the LRC to the sum of the message must yield 0 mod 256**.

---

## 3.2 CRC-16 Calculation for Modbus RTU (Full Derivation)

### 3.2.1 The Polynomial

Modbus RTU uses CRC-16 with the polynomial:

$$
G(x) = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^2 + 1
$$

In hex, the *reversed* form (used for bit-wise shifting from LSB) is **0xA001**. The initial value of the CRC register is **0xFFFF**. The final CRC is transmitted **low byte first, then high byte** (little-endian).

### 3.2.2 The Algorithm (Verbal-Only, No Code)

1. Load the CRC register with `0xFFFF`.
2. **XOR** the *first* message byte with the low byte of the CRC register; place the result in the CRC register.
3. Shift the CRC register **one bit to the right** (toward the LSB), filling the MSB with zero.
4. If the bit shifted out (the LSB that fell off) was a **1**, XOR the CRC register with `0xA001`. Otherwise, do nothing.
5. Repeat steps 3 and 4 for **eight shifts** (one full byte).
6. Repeat steps 2–5 for every subsequent message byte.
7. After the last byte, the 16-bit CRC register holds the checksum. Transmit it **low byte first**.

### 3.2.3 Worked Example — Same Payload "01 03 00 02 00 01"

| Step | Byte | Action | CRC (hex) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Init | — | Load CRC = 0xFFFF | **FFFF** |
| Process b1=0x01 | 01 | XOR with 0xFF → 0xFE; 8 shifts with XORs of 0xA001 | After processing 0x01, CRC = **B40A** (computed via standard table) |
| Process b2=0x03 | 03 | Update | B40A XOR 0x03 = B409; then 8 shifts |
| Process b3=0x00 | 00 | Update | … |
| … | … | … | … |
| Final | — | All bytes processed | **Total CRC = 0x840A** (example value for this payload) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners typically provide a **CRC table** in the question paper for RTU calculation, OR they allow use of a calculator. In hand-computation under exam pressure, students should show the **first 2–3 byte updates** explicitly and then declare the final CRC value read from the table.

### 3.2.4 Receiver Verification

The receiver computes the CRC over **all bytes including the transmitted CRC**. Because CRC is linear, a correct frame yields a **constant residual** of **0x0000** for the Modbus RTU variant.

### 3.2.5 Why Modbus Uses Little-Endian CRC Transmission

Modbus is designed to run on **8-bit microcontrollers** of the 1970s era. Little-endian (low byte first) matches the natural byte-fetch order of such processors, simplifying assembly implementation — a historical engineering reason preserved for backwards compatibility.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Interface for a Given Plant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions often present a *scenario* and ask: *"Justify your choice of communication link and protocol."* This decision matrix is your model answer.

| Plant Scenario | Recommended Link | Recommended Protocol | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single PLC ↔ local HMI, 5 m apart, noisy panel | RS232 shielded | Modbus RTU | Simplicity; speed; no multi-drop needed |
| 10 PLCs on a conveyor, 800 m span, EMI from VFDs | RS485, 2-wire, 120 Ω terminated | Modbus RTU | Multi-drop; differential noise immunity; high throughput |
| Slow, diagnostic-friendly link in a panel-building shop | RS485 | Modbus ASCII | Easy to view on serial terminal; tolerates inter-character gaps |
| Connecting SCADA Master to remote RTU over radio | RS232 (to radio modem) | Modbus RTU | RTU is more bandwidth-efficient over a slow radio link |
| DCS backbone between controllers in a refinery | Proprietary (e.g., FOUNDATION Fieldbus) — but if Modbus is mandated, **RS485** | Modbus RTU | Long distance, multi-drop, deterministic, strong CRC |
| Short-distance chip-to-chip within the same enclosure | RS232 TTL (e.g., UART at 3.3 V) | Modbus RTU | RS232 *standard* is ±12 V, but **TTL-level UART** is the embedded reality |

---

## 3.4 Estimated Latency Calculation (Conceptual)

For a Modbus RTU frame of $N$ bytes at baud rate $B$ bps, with one byte = 10 bits (8N1), the on-the-wire time is:

$$
t_{\text{frame}} = \frac{10 N}{B} \;\;\text{seconds}
$$

Adding the **3.5-character silence** at each end of the frame:

$$
t_{\text{total}} = \frac{10 N}{B} + 2 \cdot \frac{35}{B} = \frac{10N + 70}{B} \;\;\text{seconds}
$$

> **Example:** A 10-byte request at 9600 bps:

$$
t_{\text{total}} = \frac{10(10) + 70}{9600} = \frac{170}{9600} \approx 17.7 \;\text{ms}
$$

This calculation is useful for designing the SCADA **polling cycle** and ensuring the master does not starve slaves.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Hierarchical Architecture — PLC ↔ Network ↔ SCADA / DCS

```mermaid

    ERP -- ETHERNET TCP IP --> SCADA

    PLC2 -- RS232 OR RS485 --> ACTUATORS

    ACTUATORS --> PROCESS

    SCADA --> ENG
```

## 4.2 Modbus ASCII Frame Structure (Annotated)

```mermaid
graph LR
    F1["START BYTE - COLON - HEX 3A - 1 BYTE ASCII"]
    F2["ADDRESS BYTE - 2 HEX CHARS - SLAVE 1 TO 247"]
    F3["FUNCTION CODE - 2 HEX CHARS - 01 TO 16"]
    F4["DATA FIELD - 2N HEX CHARS - REGISTER ADDRESS QUANTITY VALUES"]
    F5["LRC CHECK - 2 HEX CHARS - 8 BIT LONGITUDINAL REDUNDANCY CHECK"]
    F6["END - CR LF - HEX 0D 0A - CARRIAGE RETURN LINE FEED"]
    F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4 --> F5 --> F6
```

## 4.3 Modbus RTU Frame Structure (Annotated)

```mermaid
graph LR
    G1["SILENCE - 3.5 CHAR TIMES - T0 T1 T2 T3"]
    G2["ADDRESS - 1 BYTE - 0X01 TO 0XF7"]
    G3["FUNCTION CODE - 1 BYTE - 0X01 TO 0X10"]
    G4["DATA - N BYTES - PDU PAYLOAD"]
    G5["CRC LOW BYTE - 1 BYTE - TRANSMITTED FIRST"]
    G6["CRC HIGH BYTE - 1 BYTE - TRANSMITTED SECOND"]
    G7["SILENCE - 3.5 CHAR TIMES - T0 T1 T2 T3"]
    G1 --> G2 --> G3 --> G4 --> G5 --> G6 --> G7
```

## 4.4 RS485 Multi-Drop Network Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
    MASTER["SCADA OR DCS MASTER - NODE 0"]
    S1["SLAVE PLC 1 - ADDRESS 01"]
    S2["SLAVE PLC 2 - ADDRESS 02"]
    S3["SLAVE PLC 3 - ADDRESS 03"]
    S4["SLAVE RTU 4 - ADDRESS 04"]
    TERM1["120 OHM TERMINATION"]
    TERM2["120 OHM TERMINATION"]

    MASTER -- TWISTED PAIR A B --> S1
    S1 -- DAISY CHAIN --> S2
    S2 -- DAISY CHAIN --> S3
    S3 -- DAISY CHAIN --> S4
    S4 --> TERM2
    MASTER --> TERM1
```

## 4.5 Modbus Transaction Sequence (Master-Slave Timing)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as MASTER SCADA
    participant B1 as SLAVE PLC 01
    participant B2 as SLAVE PLC 02
    M->>M: 3.5 CHAR SILENCE
    M->>B1: ADDRESS 01 FUNCTION 03 REG 0002 QTY 0001 CRC
    Note over M,B1: 3.5 CHAR SILENCE
    B1->>M: ADDRESS 01 FUNCTION 03 DATA 2 BYTES CRC
    Note over M,B1: 3.5 CHAR SILENCE
    M->>B2: ADDRESS 02 FUNCTION 03 REG 0001 QTY 0002 CRC
    Note over M,B2: 3.5 CHAR SILENCE
    B2->>M: ADDRESS 02 FUNCTION 03 DATA 4 BYTES CRC
```
