<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
*List any three advantages and three limitations of Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) in industrial automation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages of PLC:**
1. **Flexibility:** Logic can be modified through software (Ladder Logic) without physical rewiring, unlike hard-wired relay panels.
2. **High Reliability:** Solid-state design yields **MTBF exceeding 100,000 hours**; operates in harsh industrial environments.
3. **Modular & Expandable:** I/O modules can be added or swapped as production requirements change.

**Limitations of PLC:**
1. **Higher Initial Cost:** For very small systems (< 10 I/O), PLCs cost more than simple relay logic.
2. **Programming Expertise:** Requires trained personnel in vendor-specific languages (e.g., Siemens TIA Portal).
3. **Vendor Lock-in:** Proprietary hardware and software limit interoperability across brands.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Explain the concept of "Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)" and "Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)" as used in evaluating automation system reliability.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):**
It is the **average operational time** between two consecutive failures of a system, measured in **hours**. A higher MTBF indicates a more reliable system. It is calculated as:

$$MTBF = \frac{\text{Total Operational Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$$

**MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):**
It is the **average time required** to diagnose, repair, and restore a failed system to working condition, also measured in **hours**. A lower MTTR indicates better maintainability.

$$MTTR = \frac{\text{Total Downtime for Repairs}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$$

**Significance:** Together, MTBF and MTTR define the **Availability (A)** of the system: $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Question Paper]**
*Compare the advantages and limitations of PLC, DCS, and SCADA systems. Discuss with suitable examples where each system is most appropriately applied in an industrial context.*

#### (a) Detailed Comparison of PLC vs. DCS vs. SCADA (7 marks)

| Parameter | PLC | DCS | SCADA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Programmable Logic Controller | Distributed Control System | Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition |
| **Primary Function** | Discrete logic control of machinery | Continuous process control (PID loops) | Centralized monitoring and remote supervision |
| **Application Domain** | Automotive assembly, packaging, material handling | Oil refineries, petrochemical plants, power generation | Water distribution, electrical grids, pipelines |
| **I/O Handling** | 20 to 2048 (extendable) | 1000 to 50,000+ | 100 to 100,000+ (distributed geographically) |
| **Architecture** | Centralized CPU with distributed I/O racks | Centralized engineering with distributed controllers | Master station + RTUs/PLCs at remote sites |
| **Response Time** | 1–10 ms (deterministic) | 50–500 ms (loop-level) | 100 ms – 5 s (communication-limited) |
| **Redundancy** | Optional, often expensive | Built-in (controller, network, power) | Application-dependent |
| **Programming Languages** | Ladder Logic, FBD, ST, SFC | FBD, SFC, structured text, advanced control blocks | Scripting, HMI configuration |
| **Initial Cost** | Low to Medium | Very High | Medium |
| **Cybersecurity Risk** | Medium | Medium | High (internet-exposed) |
| **Vendor Examples** | Siemens S7-1500, Allen-Bradley CompactLogix | Emerson DeltaV, Honeywell Experion, ABB 800xA | WinCC, Wonderware InTouch, Ignition |
| **Key Limitation** | Limited advanced process control | Vendor lock-in, long implementation | Not suitable for closed-loop control |

#### (b) Industrial Application Examples (7 marks)

**Example 1 — PLC Application: Automotive Paint Shop (Tata Motors, Pune)**

A modern automotive paint shop uses **Siemens S7-1500 PLCs** to control conveyor movement, robotic arm coordination, paint spray activation, and oven temperature sequencing. The PLC's **1 ms scan time** ensures precise synchronization of robots moving at **2 m/s**. The limitation: if continuous temperature control is needed across 200+ zones, a DCS would be more efficient.

**Example 2 — DCS Application: Crude Oil Distillation Column (BPCL Kochi Refinery)**

A refinery distillation column requires control of **temperature, pressure, flow, and level** simultaneously across hundreds of loops. The **Emerson DeltaV DCS** provides:
- 1:1 redundant controllers for **99.99% availability**.
- Advanced Process Control (APC) blocks for feedforward and cascade control.
- Centralized HMI at the operator console.

The limitation: high CAPEX (₹50+ crore) makes it uneconomical for simple discrete applications.

**Example 3 — SCADA Application: Kerala Water Authority Distribution Network**

The Kerala Water Authority uses **SCADA** to monitor reservoir levels, pump stations, and water quality across hundreds of kilometers. **RTUs (Remote Terminal Units)** at field sites transmit data to a central **Master Station** in Thiruvananthapuram via GSM/4G. Operators can remotely start/stop pumps and view historical trends.

The limitation: communication latency of **2–5 seconds** over cellular networks makes SCADA unsuitable for fast closed-loop control.

**Conclusion:** The choice between PLC, DCS, and SCADA depends on **process type (discrete/continuous/supervisory)**, **scale**, **real-time requirements**, and **budget**. Modern plants often integrate all three in a unified **ISA-95 architecture**.

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Alternative Internal Choice]**
*With the help of a block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical SCADA system. Discuss its major advantages and limitations in modern industrial automation.*

#### (a) SCADA Architecture — Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks)

A typical SCADA system has **four hierarchical layers**:

**1. Field Level (Instrumentation Layer):**
- **Sensors** (RTD, thermocouple, pressure transmitter, flow meter) measure physical variables.
- **Actuators** (valves, motors, drives) manipulate process variables.
- **RTUs (Remote Terminal Units)** or **PLCs** act as local controllers and data concentrators.

**2. Communication Layer (Data Acquisition Network):**
- Protocols: **Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, OPC-UA, Ethernet/IP**.
- Physical media: Serial (RS-485), Ethernet, fiber-optic, cellular, satellite.

**3. Supervisory Layer (SCADA Master Station):**
- **MTU (Master Terminal Unit)** with HMI software.
- **Historian** for time-series data storage.
- **Alarm engine** for event management.

**4. Enterprise Layer (Business Integration):**
- Interfaces with **ERP (SAP)**, **MES**, and reporting tools.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1["Pressure Transmitter"] --> RTU["RTU at Field Site"]
    F2["Flow Meter"] --> RTU
    F3["Temperature Sensor"] --> RTU
    F4["Level Sensor"] --> RTU
    RTU --> COMM["Communication Network Modbus DNP3 OPC-UA Cellular Ethernet"]
    COMM --> MTU["SCADA Master Station HMI Historian Alarm Engine"]
    MTU --> ENG["Control Engineer Workstation"]
    MTU --> OPS["Operator Console"]
    MTU --> ERP["ERP System Business Layer"]
    style RTU fill:#16a085,color:#ffffff
    style COMM fill:#2980b9,color:#ffffff
    style MTU fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
    style ERP fill:#8e44ad,color:#ffffff
```

#### (b) Advantages and Limitations of SCADA (7 marks)

**Major Advantages:**

1. **Centralized Remote Monitoring:** Operators can monitor thousands of geographically distributed assets from a single control room.
2. **Historical Data & Trending:** Time-series historian enables root-cause analysis and predictive maintenance.
3. **Alarm Management:** Intelligent alarm filtering reduces operator cognitive load.
4. **Cost-Effective:** Cheaper than DCS for non-critical, wide-area applications.
5. **Open Interoperability:** Modern SCADA supports OPC-UA, bridging multi-vendor devices.
6. **Improved Decision Making:** Real-time dashboards integrate with MES/ERP for business intelligence.

**Major Limitations:**

1. **Not Real-Time Control:** Communication delays of **100 ms – 5 s** make SCADA unsuitable for closed-loop control.
2. **Cybersecurity Vulnerabilities:** Internet-connected SCADA is exposed to attacks (Stuxnet, 2010 — destroyed Iranian nuclear centrifuges).
3. **Dependency on Field Devices:** SCADA cannot function without functioning PLCs/RTUs.
4. **Alarm Fatigue:** Without proper filtering, thousands of alarms overwhelm operators.
5. **High Initial Setup:** Requires RTUs, communication infrastructure, and trained engineers.
6. **Latency and Bandwidth:** Limited by cellular/satellite link quality in remote areas.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Writing only advantages without limitations** — Examiners allocate **at least 4 marks** to limitations. Always balance your answer.
> 2. **Confusing SCADA with DCS** — SCADA does **not** perform closed-loop control. DCS does. Do not interchange them.
> 3. **Skipping examples** — A 14-mark question without **at least 2 real-world examples** will lose 3–4 marks.
> 4. **No block diagram** — A 14-mark question on architecture expects a **labeled block diagram** (5 marks reserved for the figure).
> 5. **Forgetting formulas** — When discussing reliability, include **$A = MTBF/(MTBF + MTTR)$**. Generic qualitative answers lose marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **PLC** = Discrete control, fast scan time (1–10 ms), modular, vendor-locked.
- [x] **DCS** = Continuous process control, redundant, high CAPEX, advanced PID.
- [x] **SCADA** = Supervisory only, geographically distributed, not for closed-loop, high cyber risk.
- [x] **Industrial Robot** = Repetitive physical task, ±0.02 mm precision, high CAPEX, safety caging required.
- [x] **MTBF** ↑ → Reliability ↑; **MTTR** ↓ → Maintainability ↑.
- [x] **Availability Formula:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- [x] **Reliability Function:** $R(t) = e^{-t/MTBF}$.
- [x] **Payback Period:** $P = \dfrac{C_0}{S_{annual}}$.
- [x] **OEE** = Availability × Performance × Quality.
- [x] **ISA-95 Five Levels:** Field → Control → Supervisory → Operations → Enterprise.
- [x] **Industrial Robot Cost:** ₹15 lakh – ₹2 crore.
- [x] **PLC Operating Temperature:** 0°C to 60°C.
- [x] **SCADA Latency:** 100 ms – 5 s.
- [x] **Cybersecurity Example:** Stuxnet (2010).
- [x] **Always include 3+ advantages AND 3+ limitations** in 3-mark answers.
- [x] **Always include comparison table + block diagram + examples** in 14-mark answers.

**Final Exam Mantra:** *"Automation is not about replacing humans — it is about replacing human error. Know the system, know its limits, and deploy it wisely."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DCS as an Automation Tool to Support Enterprise Resources Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT831 - INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** DCS as an Automation Tool to Support Enterprise Resources Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Distributed Control System (DCS):**
A Distributed Control System is a computerised control system for a process or plant, wherein autonomous controllers are distributed throughout the system, but there is central operator supervisory control. It is architected as a hierarchical network of controllers, I/O devices, engineering stations, and operator Human-Machine Interfaces (HMIs) communicating over a high-speed, deterministic industrial network (per **ISA-95 / IEC 62264**).

**Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP):**
An Enterprise Resource Planning system is an integrated, real-time, cross-functional software platform that manages a business's internal and external resources, including production, finance, procurement, inventory, sales, quality, and human resources, by maintaining a single unified database.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The DCS–ERP integration bridges the **shop-floor (Level 0–2)** with the **business enterprise (Level 4)**, eliminating the "island of automation" syndrome and enabling true **vertical integration** as defined in **ISA-95 (Purdue Reference Model)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a modern automobile manufacturing company as a single living organism:

- **The DCS** is the **nervous system and reflexes** of the factory floor. It constantly senses the temperature of a furnace, the pressure in a pipe, the speed of a conveyor, and instantly responds (within **milliseconds**) by opening a valve, slowing a motor, or starting a pump. It does this autonomously and continuously, 24×7, 365 days a year.

- **The ERP** is the **cerebrum (strategic brain)** of the organisation. It does not care about millisecond-level valve positions; instead, it answers questions like: *"How much raw material do we have in the warehouse? Should we accept this sales order? What is the cost of production for Batch #A47 this month? Which vendor delivers the best quality?"* — questions that take **minutes to days** to answer.

- The **DCS-ERP integration** is the **spinal cord** that carries sensory information from the nervous system (DCS) up to the brain (ERP), and motor commands (production schedules, set-points) from the brain back down to the reflexes. Without this spinal cord, the brain is blind and the reflexes are uncoordinated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Frame:** The **ISA-95 Purdue Reference Model** defines **5 levels** (Level 0: Physical Process → Level 4: Enterprise/Business). DCS typically operates at **Levels 0, 1, 2**; MES (Manufacturing Execution System) at **Level 3**; ERP at **Level 4**.

## 1.3 Core Metrics & Standards

| Term | Standard Value / Definition |
|---|---|
| **Response Time of DCS** | **10–100 ms** (real-time control loop) |
| **Response Time of ERP** | **seconds to days** (transactional) |
| **Primary DCS Standard** | **ISA-88** (batch control), **ISA-95** (enterprise integration) |
| **Primary ERP Standard** | **ISA-95 Level 4**, ISO 15704 (enterprise modelling) |
| **Communication Protocol (DCS)** | **Fieldbus (PROFIBUS, FOUNDATION Fieldbus), Modbus TCP, Ethernet/IP** |
| **Communication Protocol (ERP ↔ MES)** | **OPC UA, XML, B2MML, REST/SOAP APIs** |
| **Availability Target** | **99.99% ("Four Nines")** ≈ 52.6 minutes downtime/year |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** "**D**CS = **D**ecisions in **M**illiseconds, ERP = **E**nterprise-wide **R**eporting in **P**eriods" — use this to quickly justify why the two systems are *fundamentally different* and *must be integrated carefully*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model — The Backbone of DCS-ERP Integration

The **Purdue Reference Model**, formalised by ISA-95, partitions industrial automation into **five functional levels**. DCS-ERP integration is only meaningful when you correctly identify which level each function belongs to.

| Level | Name | Typical Systems | Time Horizon | KTU Examples |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| **L0** | Physical Process | Sensors, Actuators, Motors, Valves | Continuous (ms) | RTD, Thermocouple, I/P Converter |
| **L1** | Basic Control | PLCs, DCS Controllers, Single-Loop Controllers | **100 ms – 1 s** | PID loop, Motor starter logic |
| **L2** | Supervisory Control | SCADA / HMI / DCS Engineering Station | **1 s – 1 min** | Operator console, Alarm management |
| **L3** | Manufacturing Operations (MES) | Manufacturing Execution System | **minutes – hours** | Batch tracking, OEE, Quality |
| **L4** | Enterprise / Business | ERP, SCM, CRM, BI | **hours – weeks** | SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why DCS ≠ ERP:** A common KTU pitfall is conflating the two. The DCS optimises *physical processes* in real time; the ERP optimises *business processes* in transactional time. They are complementary, not substitutable.

## 2.2 DCS Architecture — Recap for Context

A modern DCS has the following canonical structure (relevant for KTU Module 4):

- **Level 0:** Field devices — transmitters (sensors) and final control elements (valves, VFDs).
- **Level 1:** Process Controllers (e.g., ABB AC 800M, Honeywell C300, Emerson DeltaV S-series). They execute PID, sequence, and batch control.
- **Level 2:** Operator Stations (HMI), Engineering Stations, Alarm Servers, Historians. Plant-wide data is aggregated here.
- **Level 3:** Plant Information / Manufacturing Execution System — the bridge.
- **Level 4:** Corporate ERP — the business brain.

> [!NOTE]
> **In a true DCS**, the controllers are *geographically distributed* across the plant (e.g., one controller per process unit), interconnected by a **redundant high-speed control network** (e.g., 100 Mbps / 1 Gbps Ethernet), and supervised from a *central* control room. This is the architectural "distribution" that gives DCS its name.

## 2.3 ERP — Functional Modules Relevant to Industrial Automation

A KTU student must know which ERP modules *consume* data produced by a DCS:

1. **Production Planning (PP):** Generates the *Master Production Schedule (MPS)*.
2. **Material Management / Inventory (MM / IM):** Tracks raw materials and finished goods.
3. **Quality Management (QM):** Records quality measurements from the plant.
4. **Plant Maintenance (PM):** Schedules preventive maintenance based on runtime hours.
5. **Sales & Distribution (SD):** Receives order confirmations based on production output.
6. **Finance & Controlling (FI/CO):** Calculates actual production costs from raw-material consumption data.
7. **Human Resources (HR):** Allocates operators to shifts.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU answers**, always name the modules explicitly and tie each one to a specific data point that *originates in the DCS* (e.g., QM ← Lab values from LIMS; PP ← Actual production from L2 Historians).

## 2.4 Why DCS Alone is Insufficient — The Motivation for Integration

Without ERP integration, the following problems occur (this is a *favourite* KTU 14-mark question):

1. **Data Silos:** Production data locked in DCS Historian is inaccessible to Finance and Sales.
2. **Manual Data Entry Errors:** Operators manually re-enter production counts into ERP — typically a **2–5% error rate**.
3. **Latency in Decision Making:** Decisions that should take minutes take days.
4. **Inaccurate Inventory:** The system thinks there is 1000 kg in the tank, but the DCS shows 850 kg.
5. **Loss of Traceability:** Batch genealogy cannot be linked to customer complaints.
6. **Sub-optimal Scheduling:** MPS is generated without real-time equipment availability.

## 2.5 The DCS-to-ERP Data Flow — Step-by-Step Logic

The integration follows a structured, bi-directional flow:

**Upward Flow (Shop-Floor → Business):**
- Field sensor → DCS Controller (L1)
- Controller → DCS Historian (L2) — stores time-series at **1-sec granularity** typically.
- Historian / L2 → **OPC UA Server** (a vendor-neutral middleware).
- OPC UA → **MES / Level 3** — performs contextualisation (which batch, which order, which equipment).
- MES → **ERP via B2MML (Business To Manufacturing Markup Language)** or REST/IDoc interface.
- ERP updates production orders, inventory, and cost centres in **near real-time** (latency of **30 s – 5 min** is typical).

**Downward Flow (Business → Shop-Floor):**
- Sales Order in ERP (L4) → Production Order in MES (L3).
- MES breaks Production Order into *Operations* and dispatches them to DCS as *Setpoints, Recipes, or Batch Phases*.
- DCS Controller (L1) executes the recipe; the historian records actuals; the loop continues.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary for KTU:** "Contextualisation" is the act of adding *business meaning* (batch ID, order number, customer) to raw *process data* (flow, temperature). It is the single most important step performed by the MES layer.

## 2.6 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is mandatory for KTU 14-mark answers on this topic.** Memorise the boundary between layers.

| Concept | Layer | Formula / Definition | Units / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Throughput (T)** | L0 → L4 | $T = \dfrac{m}{t}$ where $m$ = mass produced, $t$ = time | kg/h, ton/day |
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | L3 | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | dimensionless (%) |
| **Availability (A)** | L3 | $A = \dfrac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | % |
| **Performance (P)** | L3 | $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Run Time}}$ | % |
| **Quality (Q)** | L3 | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}}$ | % |
| **Production Cost (C)** | L4 | $C = C_{raw} + C_{energy} + C_{labour} + C_{overhead}$ | ₹/unit |
| **Data Latency (L)** | Network | $L = L_{sensor} + L_{ctrl} + L_{hist} + L_{MES} + L_{ERP}$ | seconds |
| **Integration Loss (η)** | L2–L4 | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Data Synchronisation Rate}}{\text{Total Events}}$ | % |
| **Order-to-Cash Cycle Time** | L4 → L0 → L4 | $T_{O2C} = T_{order} + T_{prod} + T_{deliver} + T_{invoice}$ | days |
| **ISA-95 Level Count** | — | **5 levels** (L0 to L4) | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Never write `L` for latency and `L` for level in the same answer. Use $\tau$ or $T_{lat}$ for latency to avoid confusion. Examiners deduct marks for ambiguous notation.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas (Refineries):** DCS (e.g., Honeywell Experion) feeds real-time production data into SAP ERP for hydrocarbon balancing and export scheduling.
- **Pharmaceuticals (FDA-regulated):** DCS records batch parameters; ERP records material genealogy; integration provides end-to-end **21 CFR Part 11** compliance traceability.
- **Power Plants:** DCS monitors emissions; ERP manages fuel procurement and grid revenue accounting.
- **Discrete Manufacturing (Automotive):** DCS-equivalent (PLCs) reports unit counts to ERP for Just-In-Time (JIT) supply chain management.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Computing End-to-End Integration Latency

The total latency from a field event to its appearance in an ERP transaction is a **sum of serial delays** across the five ISA-95 layers.

### Given:
- Sensor sampling delay: $\tau_s = 0.05$ s
- DCS Controller scan time: $\tau_c = 0.20$ s
- Historian write period: $\tau_h = 1.00$ s
- MES polling period: $\tau_m = 10$ s
- ERP commit/transaction time: $\tau_e = 2$ s

### Derivation:

The total latency $T_{lat}$ is the cumulative time from the physical event to its persistence in the ERP database.

$$
T_{lat} = \tau_s + \tau_c + \tau_h + \tau_m + \tau_e
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
T_{lat} = 0.05 + 0.20 + 1.00 + 10 + 2 \quad [\text{seconds}]
$$

$$
T_{lat} = 13.25 \text{ s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The MES polling period ($\tau_m = 10$ s) is the **dominant term**, contributing **75.5%** of total latency. Optimisation must focus on the MES layer — typically by using *event-driven* pushes instead of *polling*.

### Fractional contribution analysis:

$$
\% \text{ contribution of MES} = \frac{\tau_m}{T_{lat}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ contribution of MES} = \frac{10}{13.25} \times 100 = 75.47\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 14-mark answers**, present this calculation as a *worked example* demonstrating that the MES layer is the integration bottleneck. This earns the "application-level" marks.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Quantifying the Value of DCS-ERP Integration via OEE Improvement

### Problem Setup:
A chemical plant has the following pre-integration and post-integration metrics over a one-month observation window:

| Metric | Pre-Integration | Post-Integration |
|---|:---:|:---:|
| Planned Production Time | **720 h** | **720 h** |
| Actual Downtime | **120 h** | **48 h** |
| Ideal Cycle Time | **0.50 h/unit** | **0.50 h/unit** |
| Total Pieces Produced | **1000 units** | **1100 units** |
| Good Pieces | **900 units** | **1080 units** |
| Actual Run Time | 600 h | 672 h |

### Step 1: Compute Availability (A)

$$
A_{pre} = \frac{720 - 120}{720} = \frac{600}{720} = 0.8333 = 83.33\%
$$

$$
A_{post} = \frac{720 - 48}{720} = \frac{672}{720} = 0.9333 = 93.33\%
$$

### Step 2: Compute Performance (P)

$$
P_{pre} = \frac{0.50 \times 1000}{600} = \frac{500}{600} = 0.8333 = 83.33\%
$$

$$
P_{post} = \frac{0.50 \times 1100}{672} = \frac{550}{672} = 0.8185 = 81.85\%
$$

### Step 3: Compute Quality (Q)

$$
Q_{pre} = \frac{900}{1000} = 0.90 = 90.00\%
$$

$$
Q_{post} = \frac{1080}{1100} = 0.9818 = 98.18\%
$$

### Step 4: Compute OEE

$$
\text{OEE}_{pre} = A_{pre} \times P_{pre} \times Q_{pre}
$$

$$
\text{OEE}_{pre} = 0.8333 \times 0.8333 \times 0.90 = 0.6250 = 62.50\%
$$

$$
\text{OEE}_{post} = A_{post} \times P_{post} \times Q_{post}
$$

$$
\text{OEE}_{post} = 0.9333 \times 0.8185 \times 0.9818 = 0.7500 = 75.00\%
$$

### Step 5: Quantify Production Gain

$$
\Delta \text{OEE} = \text{OEE}_{post} - \text{OEE}_{pre} = 75.00\% - 62.50\% = 12.50\%
$$

$$
\text{Relative Improvement} = \frac{12.50}{62.50} \times 100 = 20.00\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** World-class manufacturing benchmark is **OEE ≥ 85%** (defined by Seiichi Nakajima, father of TPM). A 20% relative OEE improvement is a *conservative yet realistic* outcome of full DCS-ERP integration, and is a strong value-justification for capital expenditure on integration projects.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Inventory Accuracy via DCS-ERP Reconciliation

The mass balance reconciliation in a storage tank illustrates how integration eliminates inventory error.

### Setup:
- Tank volumetric capacity: $V = 50$ m³
- Density of liquid: $\rho = 980$ kg/m³
- ERP-recorded inventory: $m_{ERP} = 49000$ kg
- DCS-calculated mass (from level transmitter): $h = 49.5$ m (full height), so:

### Calculation:

$$
m_{DCS} = V \times \rho = 50 \times 980 = 49000 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\text{Discrepancy} = \vert m_{ERP} - m_{DCS} \vert = 0 \text{ kg (post-integration)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Pre-integration, this same scenario routinely shows **2–5% discrepancy** due to manual entries, unrecorded transfers, and measurement drift. Post-integration with auto-reconciliation via OPC UA, the discrepancy drops to < **0.1%** (sensor accuracy limit).

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use DCS vs ERP vs MES

| Decision Question | Owned by | Time Frame | Example |
|---|:---:|:---:|---|
| Should I open this control valve by 2%? | **DCS (L1)** | ms | Reactor temperature PID control |
| Should I trip the compressor? | **DCS (L1/L2)** | < 1 s | Emergency shutdown logic |
| Which batch is on the reactor right now? | **MES (L3)** | minutes | Operator display, SFC tracking |
| Is the equipment due for maintenance? | **MES (L3)** | hours | Predictive maintenance trigger |
| Should we accept this sales order? | **ERP (L4)** | days | ATP (Available-To-Promise) check |
| How much raw material to procure? | **ERP (L4)** | weeks | MRP (Material Requirements Planning) run |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 14-mark questions**, draw this matrix as a *table* in your answer sheet. It instantly demonstrates to the examiner that you understand the **division of responsibility** — a 2-mark bonus that most students miss.
